cards.question: What are the eligibility requirements to obtain a Private Pilot Certificate for airplane single-engine land?
cards.answer: You must be at least 17 years old, read/speak/understand English, hold at least a 3rd-class medical (or BasicMed), pass the knowledge test, accumulate the required flight hours (minimum 40 total, 20 dual, 10 solo), and pass a practical test. The age minimum is 16 for glider and balloon.

cards.question: What privileges does a Private Pilot Certificate grant, and what is the primary limitation?
cards.answer: A private pilot may act as PIC of an aircraft for which they are rated, carry passengers, and fly at night. The primary limitation is that a private pilot may not act as PIC for compensation or hire. There are limited exceptions, such as sharing operating expenses equally with passengers (fuel, oil, airport fees, rental fees) and certain charitable/community events under 14 CFR 61.113.

cards.question: Under 14 CFR 61.113, what expenses may a private pilot share with passengers?
cards.answer: A private pilot may share the pro-rata (equal) share of fuel, oil, airport expenditures, and aircraft rental fees with passengers, provided the pilot pays at least their equal share. The flight must have a common purpose — the pilot cannot be flying solely for the benefit of the passengers. This prevents private pilots from effectively selling flights while technically 'sharing' costs.

cards.question: What is required for a private pilot to act as PIC carrying passengers?
cards.answer: Within the preceding 90 days, the pilot must have made at least 3 takeoffs and 3 landings as the sole manipulator of the controls in an aircraft of the same category, class, and type (if a type rating is required). For carrying passengers at night, the 3 takeoffs and landings must have been made during the period from 1 hour after sunset to 1 hour before sunrise, and each landing must have been to a full stop.

cards.question: What is a flight review and how often is it required?
cards.answer: A flight review (formerly called a BFR) is required every 24 calendar months to act as PIC. It consists of a minimum of 1 hour of flight training and 1 hour of ground training, covering the current general operating and flight rules of Part 91 and maneuvers at the discretion of the instructor. Completing a pilot proficiency check or a new certificate/rating also satisfies the flight review requirement.

cards.question: What are the duration limits for each class of medical certificate for a pilot under age 40?
cards.answer: For a pilot under 40: a 1st-class medical is valid for 12 calendar months (for ATP privileges), a 2nd-class medical for 12 calendar months (for commercial privileges), and a 3rd-class medical for 60 calendar months (5 years) for private pilot privileges. After age 40, the 1st-class drops to 6 months, and the 3rd-class drops to 24 calendar months.

cards.question: What are the duration limits for medical certificates for a pilot age 40 and over?
cards.answer: For a pilot 40 or older: a 1st-class medical is valid for 6 calendar months (ATP privileges), a 2nd-class medical for 12 calendar months (commercial privileges), and a 3rd-class medical for 24 calendar months (private/recreational privileges). These shorter durations reflect the increased health monitoring needed as pilots age.

cards.question: What is BasicMed and what are its limitations?
cards.answer: BasicMed is an alternative to holding a traditional FAA medical certificate. A pilot must hold a valid driver's license, have held any FAA medical certificate after July 15, 2006, complete a medical education course every 24 months, and get a physical exam from a state-licensed physician every 48 months. Limitations: no more than 6 passengers, aircraft max 6,000 lbs, no flying above 18,000 ft MSL, no faster than 250 KIAS, and no compensation or hire.

cards.question: What documents must a pilot carry when acting as PIC?
cards.answer: A pilot must carry a valid pilot certificate, a valid photo ID (government-issued), and a current medical certificate (or comply with BasicMed). The medical certificate is not required if operating under BasicMed or in aircraft not requiring one (e.g., gliders, balloons). These items must be presented upon request by the FAA, NTSB, or any law enforcement officer.

cards.question: What are the VFR cruising altitudes required under 14 CFR 91.159?
cards.answer: When flying VFR above 3,000 ft AGL, cruising altitudes are based on magnetic course. On magnetic courses of 0°–179° (eastbound), fly at odd thousands plus 500 ft (e.g., 3,500, 5,500, 7,500). On magnetic courses of 180°–359° (westbound), fly at even thousands plus 500 ft (e.g., 4,500, 6,500). This provides 1,000 ft separation between VFR traffic in opposite directions.

cards.question: What equipment is required for VFR day flight under 14 CFR 91.205(b)?
cards.answer: The mnemonic A-TOMATO-FLAMES helps remember required VFR day instruments/equipment: Airspeed indicator, Tachometer (for each engine), Oil pressure gauge, Manifold pressure gauge (for altitude engines), Altimeter, Temperature gauge (for liquid-cooled engines), Oil temperature gauge, Fuel gauge, Landing gear position indicator, Anti-collision lights, Magnetic compass, ELT, Seatbelts. These minimums apply to all powered civil aircraft for VFR day.

cards.question: What additional equipment is required for VFR night flight beyond VFR day requirements?
cards.answer: The mnemonic FLAPS covers the additional equipment for VFR night: Fuses (one spare set or three fuses for each kind), Landing light (if the aircraft is operated for hire), Anti-collision lights (already required for day), Position lights (nav lights — red/green/white), Source of electrical energy (adequate for all installed electrical equipment). These additions help with visibility and safety during nighttime operations.

cards.question: When must a transponder with Mode C (altitude reporting) be operational?
cards.answer: A Mode C transponder is required in Class A, B, and C airspace; within 30 NM of a Class B primary airport (the Mode C veil); above 10,000 ft MSL (excluding at or below 2,500 ft AGL); and when flying into, within, or across the ADIZ. This ensures ATC can identify and separate traffic in busy or high-altitude airspace. Exceptions exist with ATC approval.

cards.question: What is the maximum speed below 10,000 ft MSL in the United States?
cards.answer: Below 10,000 ft MSL, the maximum indicated airspeed is 250 knots (14 CFR 91.117). Within 4 NM of a Class C or D airport at or below 2,500 ft AGL, the limit is 200 knots. Below a Class B shelf or in a VFR corridor, the limit is also 200 knots. These limits reduce closing speeds and give pilots more time to see and avoid other traffic.

cards.question: What are the minimum VFR fuel reserve requirements for day and night?
cards.answer: For day VFR, you must have enough fuel to fly to the first point of intended landing and then fly for 30 minutes at normal cruise speed. For night VFR, the reserve increases to 45 minutes at normal cruise. These reserves account for unforecast headwinds, diversions, and navigation errors.

cards.question: What are a pilot's responsibilities regarding preflight action under 14 CFR 91.103?
cards.answer: The PIC must become familiar with all available information concerning the flight. For any flight: weather reports/forecasts, fuel requirements, alternatives if the planned flight cannot be completed, and known traffic delays. For IFR or flights not in the vicinity of an airport: runway lengths, takeoff and landing distance data, and aircraft performance data including density altitude. This regulation is intentionally broad to ensure thorough preparation.

cards.question: Who has the final authority and responsibility for the operation of an aircraft?
cards.answer: The pilot in command (PIC) is directly responsible for and is the final authority as to the operation of the aircraft. In an emergency requiring immediate action, the PIC may deviate from any rule to the extent required to meet that emergency. If asked, a written report must be sent to the FAA. This authority exists because the PIC is in the best position to assess real-time conditions.

cards.question: When is a pilot required to report an accident or incident to the NTSB?
cards.answer: The NTSB must be notified immediately for aircraft accidents (substantial damage or serious/fatal injury) and certain incidents (flight control system failure, in-flight fire, in-flight collision, etc.) per NTSB Part 830. A written report must be filed within 10 days for accidents and when requested for incidents. 'Immediately' means by the most expeditious means available.

cards.question: What alcohol-related restrictions apply to pilots under 14 CFR 91.17?
cards.answer: A pilot may not act as a crewmember within 8 hours of consuming any alcoholic beverage ('8 hours bottle to throttle'), while under the influence of alcohol, with a blood alcohol content of 0.04% or greater, or while using any drug that affects faculties contrary to safety. Refusal to submit to a blood alcohol test or a DUI conviction must be reported to the FAA within 60 days.

cards.question: What are the required notifications for a change of permanent address?
cards.answer: A pilot must notify the FAA Airmen Certification Branch in writing within 30 days of a change of permanent mailing address. Failure to notify makes the pilot certificate invalid for exercising privileges, even though the certificate itself is still technically 'valid.' This ensures the FAA can reach certificate holders for safety-related communications.

cards.question: Under what conditions may a private pilot act as PIC of an aircraft towing a glider?
cards.answer: The private pilot must have logged at least 100 hours of PIC time in the aircraft category/class/type used for towing, or have a logbook endorsement from an authorized instructor certifying competence, and have made at least 3 tows within the preceding 24 months. The tow plane must be at least 180 hp (or as specified). This ensures the pilot can safely handle the unique demands of glider towing.

cards.question: What are the minimum weather conditions (visibility and cloud clearance) required to operate VFR in controlled airspace (Class C, D, E below 10,000 ft)?
cards.answer: In Class C, D, and E airspace below 10,000 ft MSL: flight visibility must be at least 3 statute miles, and cloud clearances must be 500 ft below, 1,000 ft above, and 2,000 ft horizontal. These minimums ensure pilots can see and avoid traffic while ATC provides separation. Class E above 10,000 ft MSL requires 5 SM and 1,000/1,000/1 SM clearance.

cards.question: What is Special VFR and when can it be requested?
cards.answer: Special VFR is a clearance that allows VFR flight within a surface area of controlled airspace (Class B, C, D, or E) when weather is below basic VFR minimums but at least 1 SM visibility and clear of clouds. It must be requested by the pilot and granted by ATC. At night, the pilot must be instrument rated and the aircraft instrument equipped. Some airports prohibit SVFR (listed in Part 91 Appendix D).

cards.question: What logbook entries must a student pilot have before making a solo cross-country flight?
cards.answer: A student pilot must have instructor endorsements for solo flight in the specific make/model, for solo cross-country flight, and for each specific cross-country route (or within a 50 NM area). The instructor must have reviewed the student's flight planning and determined the student is proficient for the planned flight. These layered endorsements ensure incrementally supervised independence.

cards.question: Under 14 CFR 91.211, when is supplemental oxygen required for the flight crew and passengers?
cards.answer: Flight crew must use supplemental oxygen for any time spent above 14,000 ft MSL cabin pressure altitude, and for any period exceeding 30 minutes above 12,500 ft up to and including 14,000 ft. Above 15,000 ft cabin pressure altitude, each occupant (passengers included) must be provided supplemental oxygen. These rules address the progressive impairment from hypoxia at altitude.

cards.question: What are the requirements for operating near other aircraft under 14 CFR 91.111?
cards.answer: No person may operate an aircraft so close to another aircraft as to create a collision hazard. Formation flight requires prior arrangement with the PIC of each aircraft. No person may operate an aircraft carrying passengers for hire in formation. These rules recognize that close proximity increases collision risk dramatically, so formation flight requires explicit coordination.

cards.question: What minimum flight time and experience is required for a Private Pilot Certificate (airplane)?
cards.answer: Under Part 61: minimum 40 hours total (35 under Part 141), including 20 hours of dual instruction and 10 hours of solo flight. Solo requirements include 5 hours of cross-country, one solo cross-country of at least 150 NM with full-stop landings at 3 points and one leg of at least 50 NM, 3 solo takeoffs/landings at a towered airport, and 3 hours of night flight including a 100 NM night cross-country.

cards.question: When are position lights (navigation lights) required?
cards.answer: Position lights are required from sunset to sunrise. Anti-collision lights (beacon/strobes) are required on all aircraft during all operations, though they can be turned off if the PIC determines they are a safety hazard (e.g., in clouds causing disorientation). Nav lights are: red on the left wing, green on the right wing, and white on the tail.

cards.question: What is the definition of 'night' for logging night flight time versus night-currency requirements?
cards.answer: For logging flight time, 'night' is the period from the end of evening civil twilight to the beginning of morning civil twilight. For night-currency (carrying passengers), the 3 takeoffs and landings must be made during the period from 1 hour after sunset to 1 hour before sunrise. The currency window is wider than the logging window to provide extra margin.

cards.question: What is the maximum demonstrated crosswind component, and is it a regulatory limitation?
cards.answer: The maximum demonstrated crosswind is a value shown in the POH that the manufacturer demonstrated during certification testing. It is not a regulatory limitation — it simply means crosswinds above that value were not tested. Pilots should use it as guidance; operating beyond it is legal but may exceed the pilot's or aircraft's capability. Personal minimums should account for skill level.

cards.question: Can a private pilot log instrument time in actual IMC without an instrument rating?
cards.answer: A private pilot without an instrument rating may not file or fly IFR. However, if they inadvertently encounter IMC, the time spent in actual instrument conditions can be logged as actual instrument time. A safety pilot is required for logging simulated instrument time. The key distinction is that logging time and exercising privileges are different — the pilot still violated regulations if entering IMC intentionally without a rating.

cards.question: What actions must a pilot take when deviating from an ATC clearance in an emergency?
cards.answer: Under 14 CFR 91.3, a PIC may deviate from any rule in an emergency requiring immediate action. If given priority by ATC, the pilot may be asked to submit a written report within 48 hours to the ATC facility manager. If the deviation was from a rule of Part 91, the FAA may request a report. The pilot should notify ATC as soon as possible about the deviation and its nature.

cards.question: What are the requirements for a safety pilot?
cards.answer: A safety pilot is required when the pilot flying is under simulated instrument conditions (view-limiting device). The safety pilot must hold at least a private pilot certificate with appropriate category and class ratings, occupy a seat with adequate visibility and flight controls, and hold a current medical certificate. The safety pilot is considered a required crewmember and may log SIC time.

cards.question: What does 14 CFR 91.119 say about minimum safe altitudes?
cards.answer: Except when necessary for takeoff/landing: over congested areas, you must fly at least 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within a horizontal radius of 2,000 ft. Over non-congested areas, at least 500 ft above the surface, except over open water or sparsely populated areas where you may not operate closer than 500 ft to any person, vessel, vehicle, or structure. Anywhere, high enough to make an emergency landing without undue hazard.

cards.question: What happens to a higher-class medical certificate as it ages?
cards.answer: Medical certificates cascade down in privilege as they age. A 1st-class medical drops to 3rd-class privileges after the 1st-class period expires (6 or 12 months depending on age). It does not become invalid — it continues to authorize lower-level privileges for the remaining duration. For example, a 1st-class medical for a pilot over 40 is good for 6 months of ATP privileges, then serves as a 3rd-class for up to 24 calendar months total.

cards.question: What are the requirements for aerobatic flight under Part 91?
cards.answer: Aerobatic flight is prohibited over congested areas, over an open-air assembly, within controlled airspace of a Class B/C/D/E surface area, below 1,500 ft AGL, and when flight visibility is less than 3 SM. Parachutes are required for each occupant if the bank angle exceeds 60° or pitch exceeds 30° from level (with limited exceptions). These rules protect people on the ground and ensure adequate recovery altitude.

cards.question: What documents must be on board an aircraft for it to be legal to fly?
cards.answer: The mnemonic AROW covers: Airworthiness certificate (must be displayed), Registration certificate, Operating limitations (POH/AFM or placards), Weight and balance data. All four must be physically in the aircraft. The airworthiness certificate has no expiration date as long as the aircraft is maintained properly. Registration is valid for 7 years (since 2010 re-registration program).

cards.question: What is the difference between a Standard and Special Airworthiness Certificate?
cards.answer: A Standard Airworthiness Certificate is issued for type-certificated aircraft in the normal, utility, acrobatic, commuter, or transport categories and remains valid indefinitely as long as maintenance is performed. A Special Airworthiness Certificate covers experimental, restricted, limited, provisional, and light-sport aircraft, and typically has operational limitations. Standard certificates require compliance with approved type design.

cards.question: What inspections are required to keep an aircraft airworthy, and what are their intervals?
cards.answer: The mnemonic AV1ATES helps: Annual inspection (every 12 calendar months), VOR check (IFR only, every 30 days), 100-hour inspection (if for hire/instruction), Altimeter/pitot-static (IFR only, every 24 calendar months), Transponder (every 24 calendar months), ELT (every 12 calendar months for battery/operation, battery replacement at 50% useful life or 1 hour cumulative use), ADs (Airworthiness Directives as required).

cards.question: What is the difference between an annual and a 100-hour inspection?
cards.answer: Both inspections are identical in scope — a thorough check of the entire aircraft. The difference is when they're required: an annual is required for all aircraft every 12 calendar months, while a 100-hour is additionally required when an aircraft is used for hire or flight instruction. Only an IA (Inspection Authorization holder) can perform an annual, but an A&P can perform a 100-hour. The 100-hour may be exceeded by up to 10 hours for ferrying to a place of inspection.

cards.question: What is an Airworthiness Directive (AD), and what are the compliance requirements?
cards.answer: An AD is a legally enforceable regulation issued by the FAA when an unsafe condition exists in a product (aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance). Compliance is mandatory — an aircraft is not airworthy if an applicable AD has not been complied with. ADs may be emergency (immediate compliance), one-time, or recurring. They are the FAA's method of mandating corrections to known safety problems.

cards.question: How does a pilot determine if an aircraft with an inoperative instrument can legally fly?
cards.answer: Follow the procedure in 14 CFR 91.213(d) for aircraft without an MEL: (1) Check if the item is required by the type certificate or equipment list. (2) Check if it's required by 91.205 for the type of flight. (3) Check if it's required by an AD. (4) Check if it's required by any other regulation for the specific kind of operation. If not required by any of these, the inoperative instrument must be removed or deactivated and placarded 'INOPERATIVE,' and a logbook entry made.

cards.question: What is a Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and how does it differ from the KOEL?
cards.answer: An MEL is a document approved by the FAA that lists equipment that may be inoperative under certain conditions while the aircraft remains airworthy. It is aircraft-specific and derived from the Master MEL (MMEL). The KOEL (Kinds of Operations Equipment List) is published in the POH/AFM and lists equipment required for specific types of operations (VFR day, VFR night, IFR). An MEL takes precedence over 91.213(d) procedures when one is issued for the aircraft.

cards.question: What preventive maintenance may a private pilot perform on an aircraft they own or operate?
cards.answer: Part 43 Appendix A lists simple or minor preservation operations a pilot may perform, including: replacing/servicing spark plugs, oil changes, tire replacement, wheel bearing servicing, replacing prefabricated fuel lines, cleaning and replacing air filters, replacing bulbs and reflectors, and similar tasks. The pilot must have a private certificate or higher, the aircraft must not be operated under Part 121/135, and the work must be logged in the aircraft maintenance records.

cards.question: When must an ELT be inspected and when must its battery be replaced?
cards.answer: An ELT must be inspected within 12 calendar months for proper installation, battery corrosion, operation of controls, and signal strength. The battery must be replaced (or recharged for rechargeable batteries) when 50% of its useful life has expired, or when the transmitter has been in use for more than 1 cumulative hour. ELTs transmit on 406 MHz and 121.5 MHz and must be registered with NOAA.

cards.question: When is an aircraft not required to have an ELT?
cards.answer: Exceptions include: training flights within 50 NM of the departure airport, aircraft engaged in design/testing, new aircraft being ferried with a special flight permit, agricultural operations, scheduled air carriers under Parts 121/135, and aircraft equipped with a cockpit voice recorder (which has different requirements). If an ELT is removed for maintenance, the aircraft may fly for up to 90 days without it.

cards.question: What maintenance records must be kept and for how long?
cards.answer: Records of maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations must be kept until the work is repeated, superseded, or for at least 1 year. Records for the overhaul of airframe, engine, propeller, and appliances must be retained until the work is repeated or superseded. The aircraft total time in service, current status of applicable ADs, and current status of life-limited parts must be transferred with the aircraft at sale.

cards.question: What is a Special Flight Permit and when is it needed?
cards.answer: A Special Flight Permit (ferry permit) allows an aircraft that doesn't currently meet airworthiness requirements to fly to a location for repairs, storage, or delivery. It's issued by the FAA (typically the local FSDO) and specifies the route, conditions, and limitations. Common uses include flying to an annual inspection location or ferrying an aircraft with a known deficiency to a repair facility.

cards.question: How often must the transponder be tested and inspected?
cards.answer: Transponders must be tested and inspected every 24 calendar months by an authorized repair station or appropriately rated technician. The test verifies proper reply on all modes, correct altitude reporting, and adequate signal strength. Without a current transponder check, the aircraft cannot be operated in airspace requiring a transponder (Class A, B, C, and above 10,000 ft MSL).

cards.question: What is a Supplemental Type Certificate (STC)?
cards.answer: An STC is an FAA-approved modification to an existing type-certificated aircraft, engine, or propeller. It allows alterations not anticipated by the original manufacturer, such as installing a new avionics suite or a different engine. The STC becomes part of the aircraft's permanent records and must be listed in the aircraft documentation. Any maintenance related to the STC must follow its specific instructions.

cards.question: Who may sign off (approve for return to service) an aircraft after an annual inspection?
cards.answer: Only a mechanic holding an Inspection Authorization (IA) may approve an aircraft for return to service after an annual inspection. A standard A&P mechanic can perform the work and sign off a 100-hour inspection but cannot sign off an annual. This extra level of authorization exists because the annual is the baseline check that determines overall airworthiness for all aircraft.

cards.question: What is the significance of the '100-hour inspection may be exceeded by 10 hours' rule?
cards.answer: An aircraft requiring a 100-hour inspection may be operated up to 10 hours beyond the 100-hour limit for the sole purpose of flying to a location where the inspection can be performed. However, the excess time must be included in computing the next 100-hour interval. So if you fly 108 hours to reach the shop, the next 100-hour is due at 208, not 210. This prevents operators from routinely padding the interval.

cards.question: What is a progressive inspection program?
cards.answer: A progressive inspection program is an FAA-approved alternative to the annual/100-hour inspection system. It divides the total aircraft inspection into segments performed at regular intervals, so the entire aircraft is inspected over a defined cycle (typically 12 months). The advantage is that the aircraft is never out of service for a single large inspection. It must be approved by the local FSDO.

cards.question: What are the recording requirements after completing a maintenance action?
cards.answer: Each person performing maintenance must make an entry in the maintenance record containing: a description of the work performed, the date of completion, the name of the person performing the work, their certificate number, and signature with approval for return to service. If the aircraft is found unairworthy, a written list of discrepancies must be given to the owner/operator and recorded.

cards.question: What is the role of a type certificate data sheet (TCDS)?
cards.answer: A TCDS is an FAA document that lists the conditions and limitations under which an aircraft was type-certificated. It includes information such as engine make/model, operating limitations, weight limits, CG range, fuel type, and required equipment. If an item is listed in the TCDS as required, it must be installed and functional for the aircraft to be airworthy. TCD sheets are publicly available from the FAA.

cards.question: What are the four layers of the atmosphere, and in which do most weather phenomena occur?
cards.answer: The four layers are troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, and thermosphere. Most weather occurs in the troposphere, which extends from the surface to approximately 36,000 ft at mid-latitudes (higher at the equator, lower at the poles). Temperature decreases with altitude in the troposphere at about 2°C per 1,000 ft (standard lapse rate). The tropopause marks the boundary where temperature stops decreasing.

cards.question: What is the standard atmosphere at sea level?
cards.answer: Standard atmosphere at sea level is 15°C (59°F), 29.92 inHg (1013.2 mb), and a lapse rate of approximately 2°C per 1,000 ft. Standard pressure decreases approximately 1 inHg per 1,000 ft of altitude gain near the surface. These values serve as a baseline reference for all aircraft performance calculations and altimeter calibration.

cards.question: How does the Coriolis force affect wind patterns in the Northern Hemisphere?
cards.answer: The Coriolis force deflects moving air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere (left in the Southern). This causes wind around a low-pressure system to circulate counterclockwise and inward, and around a high-pressure system to circulate clockwise and outward. The Coriolis force results from the Earth's rotation and increases with wind speed and latitude. It is zero at the equator.

cards.question: What causes wind, and what determines its speed and direction?
cards.answer: Wind is caused by pressure differences (pressure gradient force) — air flows from high to low pressure. Wind speed is proportional to the pressure gradient: closely spaced isobars mean stronger winds. Near the surface, friction slows the wind and causes it to flow across isobars toward low pressure at about a 30° angle. At upper levels above friction, wind flows parallel to isobars (geostrophic wind) due to balance between pressure gradient and Coriolis forces.

cards.question: What is the relationship between temperature, pressure, and air density?
cards.answer: As temperature increases, air density decreases (air expands). As pressure decreases, air density decreases. As humidity increases, air density decreases (water vapor is lighter than dry air). Lower density means reduced aircraft performance because the wings produce less lift and the engine produces less power. This relationship is critical for understanding density altitude and aircraft performance.

cards.question: What are the characteristics of a stable atmosphere versus an unstable atmosphere?
cards.answer: Stable air: stratiform clouds, steady precipitation (drizzle/rain), smooth air with poor visibility in haze/fog, restricted visibility. Unstable air: cumuliform clouds, showery precipitation, turbulence, good visibility outside of precipitation. Stability depends on the environmental lapse rate — if it's less than the adiabatic lapse rate, the atmosphere is stable; if greater, it's unstable. A temperature inversion is an extremely stable condition.

cards.question: How do warm fronts and cold fronts differ in terms of weather produced?
cards.answer: A cold front has a steep frontal slope with a narrow band of weather: cumuliform clouds, heavy rain/thunderstorms, turbulence, and rapid weather changes. A warm front has a shallow slope producing widespread stratiform clouds, steady precipitation, low ceilings, and poor visibility extending well ahead of the surface front. Cold fronts move faster (typically 25-30 mph) than warm fronts (10-15 mph), and cold front weather passes more quickly.

cards.question: What are the three stages of a thunderstorm lifecycle?
cards.answer: Cumulus (building) stage: characterized by updrafts only, building cumulus clouds, no precipitation at the surface. Mature stage: the most dangerous — both updrafts and downdrafts present, heavy rain/hail, lightning, strongest turbulence, and microbursts possible; begins when precipitation reaches the ground. Dissipating stage: predominantly downdrafts, light rain, anvil shape forms, storm weakens. A single cell typically lasts 20-90 minutes.

cards.question: What hazards are associated with thunderstorms for aviation?
cards.answer: Thunderstorm hazards include severe turbulence, wind shear and microbursts, hail (can occur in clear air near the storm), lightning, icing (rapid accumulation in cumulonimbus), tornadoes, low-level wind shear on approach/departure, and reduced visibility. Microbursts are particularly dangerous near the surface because they produce rapidly changing headwinds/tailwinds. Pilots should avoid thunderstorms by at least 20 NM.

cards.question: What is a microburst and why is it dangerous to aircraft?
cards.answer: A microburst is a localized, intense downdraft that spreads outward upon hitting the surface, creating rapidly changing wind conditions. It can produce headwinds changing to tailwinds of up to 45 knots within seconds. An aircraft on approach encountering a microburst first experiences increasing headwind (increased performance), then a downdraft and increasing tailwind (rapidly decreasing performance). Microbursts last 5-15 minutes and cover less than 2.5 miles.

cards.question: What are the three types of structural icing and what conditions produce them?
cards.answer: Clear ice: forms in large supercooled water droplets or freezing rain; heavy, hard to remove, changes airfoil shape. Rime ice: forms in small supercooled droplets; rough, milky, opaque; disrupts airflow. Mixed ice: combination of clear and rime, has rough surface with some clear areas. Icing occurs in visible moisture (clouds or precipitation) when the temperature is between 0°C and -20°C. Most severe icing occurs near 0°C with large droplets.

cards.question: What conditions are necessary for carburetor icing, and when is it most likely?
cards.answer: Carburetor icing can occur at outside air temperatures as warm as 70°F (21°C) and humidity as low as 50%, because the venturi effect and fuel vaporization can drop the temperature inside the carburetor by up to 70°F. It is most likely at temperatures between 20°F and 70°F with high humidity. Reduced power settings (such as descent) are especially susceptible because the throttle plate is partially closed, creating more venturi effect. Signs include RPM drop (fixed pitch) or manifold pressure drop (constant speed).

cards.question: What are the different types of fog and how do they form?
cards.answer: Radiation fog: forms on clear, calm nights when the ground cools and chills the air above it to the dewpoint. Advection fog: warm, moist air moves over a colder surface (common along coastlines). Upslope fog: moist air is forced up a slope and cools adiabatically. Steam fog: cold air moves over warm water. Precipitation-induced fog: warm rain falls through cool air, evaporating and saturating it. Radiation fog typically burns off after sunrise; advection fog can persist.

cards.question: What is a temperature inversion and why is it significant for pilots?
cards.answer: A temperature inversion occurs when temperature increases with altitude instead of the normal decrease. It creates an extremely stable layer that traps pollutants, haze, and fog below it, causing very poor surface visibility. Wind shear can occur at the inversion boundary. Inversions commonly form on clear, calm nights (surface-based radiation inversion) and can also form aloft due to frontal activity. Low-level inversions often produce smooth air with restricted visibility.

cards.question: What is the dewpoint and its significance in weather forecasting?
cards.answer: The dewpoint is the temperature to which air must be cooled (at constant pressure) to reach saturation. When the temperature-dewpoint spread is small (within 2-3°C), fog or low clouds are likely. A converging temperature and dewpoint indicates increasing moisture and cloud formation. In rising air, the spread decreases at about 2.5°C per 1,000 ft, which helps estimate cloud base altitude: cloud base (ft AGL) ≈ (temp - dewpoint) / 2.5 × 1,000.

cards.question: What are the characteristics of an occluded front?
cards.answer: An occluded front forms when a faster-moving cold front catches up to a warm front, lifting the warm air off the surface. A cold-type occlusion has colder air behind the cold front; a warm-type has colder air ahead. Weather associated with occluded fronts includes wide areas of clouds and precipitation combining characteristics of both warm and cold fronts, with potentially severe weather at the point of occlusion. This is often the most complex frontal weather.

cards.question: What is wind shear and at what altitudes is it most hazardous?
cards.answer: Wind shear is a sudden change in wind speed and/or direction over a short distance. It is most hazardous at low altitudes during takeoff and landing, where there is little margin for recovery. Common sources: thunderstorm microbursts, frontal passage, temperature inversions, and strong surface heating creating thermals. Low-level wind shear (below 2,000 ft AGL) can cause sudden changes in airspeed and flight path, potentially resulting in terrain contact.

cards.question: What causes convective turbulence and when is it most prevalent?
cards.answer: Convective turbulence is caused by uneven surface heating, which creates rising columns of warm air (thermals) and compensating downdrafts. It is strongest on hot, sunny afternoons over dark surfaces like plowed fields or asphalt. Cumulus clouds mark the top of thermals. Convective turbulence is usually absent over water and cooler surfaces and diminishes after sunset. Flying higher can reduce its intensity as thermals spread and weaken with altitude.

cards.question: What is the significance of the tropopause and jet stream for VFR pilots?
cards.answer: The tropopause is the boundary between the troposphere and stratosphere where temperature stops decreasing. The jet stream flows near the tropopause at speeds that can exceed 100 knots. While VFR pilots rarely fly at those altitudes, jet stream effects include: clear air turbulence (CAT) near the jet stream, strong upper-level winds affecting weather system movement, and wind shear. Understanding jet stream position helps predict surface weather development.

cards.question: How do mountain waves form, and what hazards do they present?
cards.answer: Mountain waves form when strong, stable winds blow perpendicular to a mountain range. Air is forced up and over the terrain, creating oscillating waves downwind that can extend well above the peaks. Hazards include severe turbulence, rotor turbulence at lower levels (beneath the crests), strong updrafts and downdrafts, and lenticular clouds. Pilots should cross mountain ridges at 45° angles to allow a quick escape and maintain at least 50% more than minimum obstacle clearance altitude.

cards.question: What is a stationary front and what weather does it produce?
cards.answer: A stationary front occurs when two air masses meet but neither advances. The weather resembles a weak warm front: extended periods of clouds, light precipitation, and poor visibility along and on the cool air side of the front. It can persist for days, creating prolonged IFR conditions. When upper-level wind changes push one air mass, the stationary front begins moving as either a warm or cold front.

cards.question: What are the characteristics of high-pressure and low-pressure systems?
cards.answer: High pressure: air descends and diverges at the surface, rotating clockwise (Northern Hemisphere); associated with clear skies, good visibility, and stable conditions. Low pressure: air converges at the surface and rises, rotating counterclockwise; associated with clouds, precipitation, and unstable conditions. Remember: 'Low is lousy' weather. Fronts extend outward from low-pressure centers.

cards.question: How does the adiabatic lapse rate help predict cloud formation?
cards.answer: Unsaturated (dry) air cools at the dry adiabatic lapse rate of 3°C per 1,000 ft as it rises. Once it reaches the dewpoint, condensation begins (cloud base) and the air continues cooling at the moist (saturated) adiabatic lapse rate of about 1.5-2°C per 1,000 ft (slower because latent heat is released during condensation). Comparing these rates to the environmental lapse rate determines if rising air will continue to rise (unstable) or resist vertical motion (stable).

cards.question: What type of clouds indicate unstable air and potential turbulence?
cards.answer: Cumuliform clouds (cumulus, towering cumulus, cumulonimbus) indicate unstable air with vertical development and convective activity. They form from rising air parcels and are associated with turbulence, gusty winds, and showery precipitation. Cumulonimbus (CB) clouds specifically indicate thunderstorms. In contrast, stratiform clouds (stratus, altostratus) indicate stable air with smooth flying conditions but possibly poor visibility.

cards.question: What cloud types are associated with each altitude level?
cards.answer: Low clouds (surface to 6,500 ft): stratus, stratocumulus, nimbostratus. Middle clouds (6,500–20,000 ft): altostratus, altocumulus — prefixed with 'alto.' High clouds (20,000+ ft): cirrus, cirrostratus, cirrocumulus — composed of ice crystals, prefixed with 'cirro.' Clouds with significant vertical development (towering cumulus, cumulonimbus) can span multiple levels. Nimbo- or -nimbus indicates precipitation-producing clouds.

cards.question: How does sea breeze/land breeze circulation work?
cards.answer: During the day, land heats faster than water. Warm air over land rises, and cooler air from over the water flows in to replace it — this is a sea breeze (blowing from sea to land). At night, the land cools faster; air over the relatively warmer water rises, and air flows from land to sea — a land breeze. These local circulations are important for coastal airports and typically extend only a few miles inland.

cards.question: What are the signs of approaching thunderstorm activity?
cards.answer: Visual cues include towering cumulus or cumulonimbus clouds, anvil-shaped cloud tops, dark cloud bases, and visible lightning. Instrument cues include a rapidly falling altimeter setting, gusty/shifting surface winds, and a sudden temperature drop. On weather radar: strong echoes, hook echoes (possible tornado). ATC may issue alerts. A roll cloud or shelf cloud at the leading edge of a storm indicates strong low-level outflow and wind shear.

cards.question: How do you decode a standard METAR report?
cards.answer: METAR format: Type (METAR/SPECI), Station ID (ICAO), Date/Time (DDHHMMz), Wind (direction/speed in knots), Visibility (SM), Weather/obscurations, Cloud cover (FEW/SCT/BKN/OVC + altitude in hundreds of ft AGL), Temperature/Dewpoint (°C), Altimeter (Axxxx in inHg), Remarks. Example: 'METAR KJFK 121856Z 31015G25KT 10SM FEW250 18/04 A2990' = JFK, 12th at 1856Z, wind 310° at 15 gusting 25 kt, 10 SM visibility, few clouds at 25,000 ft, temp 18°C/dew 4°C, altimeter 29.90.

cards.question: What do the cloud cover abbreviations in a METAR mean?
cards.answer: SKC/CLR = clear (SKC is manual observation, CLR is automated with no clouds below 12,000 ft). FEW = 1-2 oktas (1/8 to 2/8 coverage). SCT (scattered) = 3-4 oktas. BKN (broken) = 5-7 oktas. OVC (overcast) = 8 oktas (complete coverage). A ceiling is the lowest layer reported as BKN or OVC (or vertical visibility into an obscuration). Heights are in hundreds of feet AGL.

cards.question: How do you decode a TAF, and how does it differ from a METAR?
cards.answer: A TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) is a forecast of weather conditions within 5 SM of an airport. It covers a 24- or 30-hour period and uses similar encoding to METARs. Key difference: TAFs forecast future conditions; METARs report current observations. TAFs include change groups: FM (from — complete change), BECMG (becoming — gradual change), TEMPO (temporary fluctuations lasting less than an hour and totaling less than half the period). Issued 4 times daily.

cards.question: What is the difference between an AIRMET, SIGMET, and Convective SIGMET?
cards.answer: AIRMET (WA): advisories for weather significant to light aircraft and VFR pilots — moderate icing, moderate turbulence, sustained surface winds >30 kt, IFR conditions, mountain obscuration. Valid 6 hours. SIGMET (WS): severe weather significant to ALL aircraft — severe icing not associated with thunderstorms, severe/extreme turbulence, volcanic ash. Valid up to 4 hours. Convective SIGMET (WST): thunderstorm-related hazards — tornadoes, lines of thunderstorms, embedded storms, hail >¾ inch. Valid 2 hours.

cards.question: What are the three AIRMET categories and what does each cover?
cards.answer: AIRMET Sierra: IFR conditions and/or mountain obscuration (ceilings below 1,000 ft and/or visibility below 3 miles, extensive mountain obscuration). AIRMET Tango: turbulence (moderate), sustained surface winds of 30 knots or greater, and low-level wind shear. AIRMET Zulu: moderate icing and freezing level heights. The alliterative names help remember: Sierra=See (visibility), Tango=Turbulence, Zulu=icing (Zreezing).

cards.question: What information does a PIREP (Pilot Report) contain and why are they valuable?
cards.answer: PIREPs contain pilot-observed conditions: type (/UA=routine, /UUA=urgent), location, time, altitude, aircraft type, sky cover, flight visibility, temperature, wind, turbulence, icing, and remarks. They are valuable because they provide real-time observations from actual aircraft in the atmosphere — the only direct report of conditions like turbulence and icing at altitude. PIREPs help verify forecasts and warn other pilots. All pilots are encouraged to file them.

cards.question: What are the three types of weather briefings available from Flight Service?
cards.answer: Standard briefing: requested when you have no previous weather information; covers all applicable weather data (adverse conditions, VFR flight not recommended, synopsis, current conditions, en route forecast, destination forecast, winds aloft, NOTAMs, ATC delays). Abbreviated briefing: when you need to update a previous briefing or need only specific items. Outlook briefing: for planned departures 6+ hours in the future; provides a general overview to help with early planning.

cards.question: What does 'VFR flight not recommended' (VNR) mean in a weather briefing?
cards.answer: When a briefer includes VNR, it means current or forecast conditions along the route are at or below VFR minimums, making the flight hazardous for VFR pilots. It is advisory only — the final go/no-go decision rests with the PIC. VNR is not a regulatory prohibition; however, ignoring it shows poor ADM. The briefer provides this alert at their professional judgment when sky conditions, visibility, or other weather make VFR flight questionable.

cards.question: What is a surface analysis chart and what information does it provide?
cards.answer: A surface analysis chart is a snapshot of current surface weather conditions including the positions of fronts, pressure systems (highs and lows), isobars, and weather observations. It is transmitted every 3 hours. It shows the big-picture weather pattern, helping pilots understand frontal positions, pressure gradients (and thus wind), and air mass boundaries. It should be used with prognostic charts to understand weather trends.

cards.question: What are prognostic (prog) charts and how are they used in flight planning?
cards.answer: Prognostic charts are forecasts of surface and upper-level weather conditions at specific future times (12, 24, 36, 48 hours). Surface prog charts show forecasted frontal positions, pressure centers, precipitation areas, and type of precipitation. They help pilots anticipate weather changes during a planned flight. Low-level significant weather prog charts show forecast turbulence, freezing levels, and IFR/MVFR conditions for the lower atmosphere.

cards.question: How are winds aloft forecasts (FB) used, and what are their limitations?
cards.answer: Winds aloft forecasts (FB) provide forecast wind direction (true north), speed, and temperature for selected altitudes (3,000, 6,000, 9,000, 12,000 ft, etc.) at reporting stations. Winds are not forecast for levels within 1,500 ft of station elevation. If the wind speed is forecast at 200 knots or more, it's shown as 'light and variable.' A code of 9900 means light and variable. They help pilots choose optimal altitudes for groundspeed and calculate fuel burn and time en route.

cards.question: How do you decode winds aloft when the direction is coded above 360?
cards.answer: When wind speed exceeds 100 knots, the direction is coded by adding 50 to the tens digit and adding 100 to the speed. For example, '7545' means: 75-50 = 25, so wind direction is 250°; 45+100 = 145, so wind speed is 145 knots. If coded '9900,' winds are light and variable. Temperatures above 24,000 ft are always negative and the minus sign is omitted. This encoding convention saves space in the forecast format.

cards.question: What does the remark 'A02' in a METAR indicate?
cards.answer: A02 means the station is an automated observation with a precipitation discriminator — it can distinguish between rain, snow, and other precipitation types. A01 means automated without a precipitation discriminator. This is important because A02 stations provide more detailed and reliable precipitation information. Most modern ASOS/AWOS-3 stations are A02. The remark appears in the RMK section of the METAR.

cards.question: What weather phenomenon is indicated by a METAR reporting 'TS' and 'CB'?
cards.answer: TS indicates thunderstorm (with or without precipitation at the time of observation), and CB indicates cumulonimbus clouds. Together they confirm active thunderstorm activity at or near the airport. The METAR may also show modifiers like -TSRA (thunderstorm with light rain) or +TSRA (thunderstorm with heavy rain). The direction and movement may be noted in remarks. These observations should prompt serious consideration of delay or diversion.

cards.question: What is a Graphical Forecast for Aviation (GFA) and what replaced it?
cards.answer: The GFA replaced the legacy textual area forecast (FA) in 2017. It is a web-based graphical display showing forecasted clouds, weather, turbulence, and icing for the continental US from the surface to FL450. It provides forecasts in 3-hour increments out to 15 hours. Pilots use it to get a broad overview of en route conditions. It's accessed through aviationweather.gov and is an important supplement to TAFs, which only cover terminal areas.

cards.question: What information is contained in NOTAM-D versus FDC NOTAMs?
cards.answer: NOTAM-D (Distant): information about airport/facility conditions like runway closures, navaid outages, airport lighting changes, and taxiway closures. These are expected by pilots to check before flight. FDC NOTAMs: issued by the FAA's Flight Data Center for regulatory information — changes to instrument approach procedures, TFRs (Temporary Flight Restrictions), and chart changes. FDC NOTAMs are particularly important as TFR violations carry serious consequences.

cards.question: What does 'MVFR' mean and what are its criteria?
cards.answer: MVFR (Marginal VFR) refers to ceiling 1,000 to 3,000 ft and/or visibility 3 to 5 SM. It represents conditions legal for VFR flight but marginal — just barely meeting or slightly exceeding VFR minimums. Flight categories are: VFR (>3,000 ft ceiling, >5 SM), MVFR (1,000-3,000 ft, 3-5 SM), IFR (<1,000 ft or <3 SM), and LIFR (Low IFR: <500 ft or <1 SM). These color-coded categories appear on many weather products.

cards.question: What is density altitude and how does it affect aircraft performance?
cards.answer: Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for nonstandard temperature. It represents the altitude at which the airplane 'thinks' it is flying in terms of performance. High density altitude (caused by high temperature, high elevation, high humidity, or low pressure) reduces engine power, propeller efficiency, and wing lift. On a hot, high-elevation day, an airplane may need significantly longer runway for takeoff and have reduced climb performance. Always compute density altitude for performance planning.

cards.question: How do you calculate pressure altitude?
cards.answer: Pressure altitude is the altitude indicated when the altimeter is set to 29.92 inHg (standard pressure). Calculate it as: field elevation + ((29.92 - current altimeter setting) × 1,000). For example, at an airport elevation of 5,000 ft with an altimeter setting of 29.72: 5,000 + ((29.92 - 29.72) × 1,000) = 5,000 + 200 = 5,200 ft pressure altitude. Pressure altitude is the starting point for density altitude and performance chart calculations.

cards.question: What factors affect takeoff distance?
cards.answer: Factors that increase takeoff distance: high density altitude, heavy weight, tailwind, uphill runway gradient, soft/wet/contaminated runway surface, high grass, and short/no flap use. Each factor either reduces acceleration (weight, drag, density altitude reducing power) or increases the ground speed needed for lift. A rule of thumb: every 1,000 ft of density altitude adds approximately 10% to takeoff distance. Weight is one of the most significant factors.

cards.question: What factors affect landing distance?
cards.answer: Factors that increase landing distance: high density altitude (higher true airspeed for the same indicated airspeed), heavy weight (more kinetic energy to dissipate), tailwind (higher groundspeed), downhill slope, wet/icy runway (reduced braking), high approach speed. Using full flaps, headwind, and uphill slope decrease landing distance. A tailwind component increases landing distance by roughly 10% per 2 knots of tailwind. Always use published performance data.

cards.question: What is the difference between Vx and Vy, and when do you use each?
cards.answer: Vx is the best angle-of-climb speed — it gains the most altitude per unit of horizontal distance. Use Vx when you need obstacle clearance after takeoff (e.g., trees at the end of a short runway). Vy is the best rate-of-climb speed — it gains the most altitude per unit of time. Use Vy for normal climbs to reach cruise altitude efficiently. Both speeds decrease with altitude; they converge at the aircraft's absolute ceiling. Vy is always faster than Vx.

cards.question: How do you perform a weight and balance calculation using the moment method?
cards.answer: For each item (empty weight, fuel, pilot, passengers, baggage), multiply weight × arm (distance from datum) = moment. Sum all weights to get total weight. Sum all moments to get total moment. Divide total moment by total weight to get CG location. Verify: (1) total weight does not exceed max gross weight, and (2) CG falls within the approved CG envelope. Both conditions must be met for the aircraft to be safe and legal to fly.

cards.question: What is the significance of the center of gravity (CG) being within limits?
cards.answer: A CG within limits ensures the aircraft is controllable and stable. Forward CG: more stable but higher stall speed, requires more back-pressure, harder to flare, increased fuel consumption. Aft CG: less stable, lighter control forces, lower stall speed, potentially unrecoverable stall/spin if too far aft. A CG beyond the aft limit is extremely dangerous because the aircraft may become uncontrollable. CG limits are established during certification testing.

cards.question: What is the 'datum' in weight and balance, and what is an 'arm'?
cards.answer: The datum is an imaginary vertical reference plane from which all horizontal distances (arms) are measured. It is established by the manufacturer and may be at the engine firewall, nose of the aircraft, or any other fixed point. An arm is the horizontal distance from the datum to the CG of an item. Arms behind the datum are positive; arms ahead are negative. The moment is weight × arm and represents the turning force about the datum.

cards.question: What is ground effect and how does it affect aircraft performance?
cards.answer: Ground effect is the reduction in induced drag when an aircraft flies within approximately one wingspan height above the surface. The ground disrupts wingtip vortices, reducing downwash and induced drag. This makes the aircraft feel like it can fly at a lower speed than it actually can. Danger: an aircraft may become airborne in ground effect before reaching a safe climb speed, then 'settle' when leaving ground effect. Also makes it easier to float during landing.

cards.question: What is best glide speed and why is it important?
cards.answer: Best glide speed (often published as VBG or VG) provides the maximum distance per altitude lost in a power-off glide. It corresponds to the best lift-to-drag ratio (L/Dmax). It is critical during engine failure: flying at best glide speed maximizes the distance you can cover to reach a landing site. It varies with weight — lighter weight requires a lower best glide speed. It is published for max gross weight in the POH.

cards.question: How does weight affect aircraft performance?
cards.answer: Increased weight increases stall speed (proportional to the square root of the weight ratio), increases takeoff and landing distances, reduces climb rate, reduces cruise speed for the same power setting, reduces range and endurance, and increases fuel consumption. Maximum gross weight is a structural and performance limit. For every 10% weight increase, expect roughly a 5% increase in stall speed and 20% increase in takeoff distance.

cards.question: How does a headwind or tailwind affect takeoff and landing performance?
cards.answer: A headwind reduces groundspeed for a given airspeed, shortening both takeoff and landing distances. A 10-knot headwind might reduce takeoff distance by about 20%. A tailwind increases groundspeed, increasing both distances significantly. A 10-knot tailwind might increase takeoff distance by about 20% and landing distance by even more due to the higher groundspeed on touchdown. Always use performance charts that account for wind component.

cards.question: What is the effect of a forward CG on aircraft performance?
cards.answer: A forward CG increases longitudinal stability but degrades several performance parameters: higher stall speed (the tail must produce more downforce, so the wing must produce more lift), increased fuel consumption (the tail downforce creates additional drag), requires more elevator authority to flare for landing, and slightly increased cruise speed due to the nose-heavy trim. The aircraft is harder to stall but may run out of elevator authority at forward CG limits.

cards.question: How do you use a takeoff performance chart from the POH?
cards.answer: Enter the chart with your pressure altitude and temperature to find the baseline distance. Apply corrections for weight (if different from chart assumptions), headwind/tailwind component, runway surface (paved vs. grass), and runway gradient. Some charts are tables; others are graphs requiring you to follow lines from one axis to the next. Always use the most conservative applicable conditions and add a safety factor. Note whether the chart gives ground roll only or distance to clear a 50-ft obstacle.

cards.question: What is service ceiling vs. absolute ceiling?
cards.answer: Service ceiling is the density altitude at which the aircraft can no longer sustain a climb rate of 100 fpm. Absolute ceiling is where the climb rate reaches 0 fpm — the highest the aircraft can fly in steady, level flight. At these altitudes, Vx and Vy converge. Performance is severely limited near the ceiling due to reduced air density. Turbocharging extends the service ceiling by maintaining sea-level manifold pressure to higher altitudes.

cards.question: How do you calculate the fuel required for a cross-country flight?
cards.answer: Determine the distance for each leg, calculate groundspeed using true airspeed adjusted for wind, then compute time per leg (distance / groundspeed). Multiply time by fuel burn rate (gallons per hour from the POH) to get fuel per leg. Sum all legs, add fuel for climb, add taxi fuel, then add the required VFR reserve (30 min day / 45 min night at normal cruise). Compare total fuel required to usable fuel capacity. If fuel required exceeds capacity, plan a fuel stop.

cards.question: What is leaning the mixture and when should it be done?
cards.answer: Leaning is reducing the fuel-to-air ratio by pulling back the mixture control. At altitude, air is less dense, so less fuel is needed for the correct ratio. Lean for cruise above 3,000 ft density altitude (or per POH). Over-rich mixture wastes fuel and fouls spark plugs. Lean until peak EGT, then enrichen slightly per POH (typically 50°F rich of peak or as specified). Never lean aggressively at high power settings — the rich mixture helps cool the engine.

cards.question: What is the effect of humidity on aircraft performance?
cards.answer: High humidity reduces performance because water vapor is less dense than dry air (water molecules are lighter than nitrogen and oxygen molecules). This means humid air has lower density, increasing density altitude. The engine also produces less power because there's less oxygen per unit of air. While humidity's effect is smaller than temperature's, it can add significantly to takeoff distance on already hot days — typically 10-15% increase at very high humidity.

cards.question: What are the V-speeds Vs0 and Vs1?
cards.answer: Vs0 is the stall speed in landing configuration (full flaps, gear down) — the bottom of the white arc on the airspeed indicator. Vs1 is the stall speed in clean configuration (flaps up, gear up) — the bottom of the green arc. Vs0 is always lower than Vs1 because flaps increase the wing's coefficient of lift, allowing slower flight. Both speeds increase with weight and bank angle. They are published for maximum gross weight.

cards.question: How does runway gradient (slope) affect takeoff and landing performance?
cards.answer: An uphill runway gradient increases takeoff distance because gravity works against acceleration, and decreases landing distance because gravity aids deceleration. A downhill gradient has the opposite effect. Even a 1-2% gradient can significantly affect distances. Generally, taking off downhill and landing uphill is preferable. When gradient and wind conflict, wind usually takes priority — but always run the numbers. Runway gradient information is in the Chart Supplement.

cards.question: What are the four forces acting on an airplane in flight?
cards.answer: Lift (opposes weight, acts perpendicular to the relative wind), Weight (acts downward toward Earth's center due to gravity), Thrust (produced by the engine/propeller, acts forward along the flight path), and Drag (opposes thrust, acts rearward parallel to the relative wind). In unaccelerated, straight-and-level flight, lift equals weight and thrust equals drag. These forces interact — changing one always affects the others.

cards.question: How is lift generated by an airplane wing?
cards.answer: Lift is generated by a combination of Bernoulli's principle and Newton's third law. The wing's shape and angle of attack cause air to flow faster over the upper surface (lower pressure) and slower under the lower surface (higher pressure), creating a pressure differential that pushes the wing upward. Simultaneously, the wing deflects air downward, and the equal-and-opposite reaction pushes the wing up. Both effects contribute to total lift.

cards.question: What is angle of attack (AOA), and how does it relate to stalls?
cards.answer: Angle of attack is the angle between the chord line of the wing and the relative wind (the direction of airflow relative to the wing). It is NOT the pitch angle. As AOA increases, lift increases up to the critical angle of attack (typically 16-20° for most general aviation airfoils). Beyond the critical AOA, airflow separates from the upper wing surface, lift decreases abruptly, and drag increases — this is a stall. A stall can occur at any airspeed, any attitude, any power setting.

cards.question: What causes an airplane to stall and how is it recovered?
cards.answer: A stall is caused by exceeding the critical angle of attack — the point where airflow can no longer follow the wing's upper surface smoothly. This can happen at any airspeed or attitude. Recovery: reduce angle of attack (push forward on the yoke/stick), apply full power (to minimize altitude loss), and level the wings (coordinated rudder/aileron). The key principle is that you must decrease AOA first — no amount of power alone will break the stall.

cards.question: What is load factor, and how does it relate to bank angle in a turn?
cards.answer: Load factor is the ratio of lift produced to the aircraft's weight, expressed in G's. In level flight, load factor is 1G. In a coordinated level turn, load factor increases with bank angle: 30° bank = 1.15G, 45° = 1.41G, 60° = 2.0G, 75° = 3.86G. Higher load factor increases stall speed (stall speed increases by the square root of the load factor). At 60° bank, stall speed is 41% higher than 1G stall speed. This is why steep turns increase stall risk.

cards.question: What is maneuvering speed (Va) and why does it change with weight?
cards.answer: Va is the maximum speed at which full, abrupt control deflection will cause the aircraft to stall before exceeding its structural limit load factor. Above Va, aggressive control inputs can overstress the airframe. Va decreases with lighter weight because a lighter aircraft reaches its limit load factor at a lower airspeed. Va is published for max gross weight — at lighter weights, use a lower Va. Rough rule: decrease Va by about 1% for every 2% decrease in weight.

cards.question: What are the four left-turning tendencies in a single-engine propeller aircraft?
cards.answer: 1) Torque reaction: Newton's third law — the engine turns the propeller clockwise, so the airframe reacts by rolling left. 2) P-factor (asymmetric thrust): at high AOA, the descending blade (right side) has a higher AOA and produces more thrust, yawing the aircraft left. 3) Spiraling slipstream: propeller wash spirals around the fuselage and hits the left side of the vertical stabilizer, pushing the tail right and nose left. 4) Gyroscopic precession: when the nose is pitched, the force acts 90° in the direction of rotation.

cards.question: What are the two types of drag and their components?
cards.answer: Parasite drag increases with speed and has three components: form drag (shape of the aircraft), skin friction drag (surface roughness), and interference drag (where components meet, e.g., wing-fuselage junction). Induced drag is a byproduct of lift production — it decreases with speed and increases at high angles of attack. Total drag is the sum of both; minimum total drag occurs at the speed for maximum L/D ratio, which is also best glide speed.

cards.question: What happens aerodynamically during a spin?
cards.answer: A spin is an aggravated stall with autorotation. One wing is more deeply stalled than the other, creating asymmetric lift and drag. The more-stalled wing drops and moves backward while the less-stalled wing rises and advances, causing rotation. Entry requires a stall AND yaw (typically from uncoordinated flight). Recovery (PARE): Power idle, Ailerons neutral, Rudder full opposite to the spin direction, Elevator forward to break the stall, then neutralize controls and recover from the dive.

cards.question: What are the three types of static stability?
cards.answer: Positive static stability: after a disturbance, the aircraft tends to return toward its original state (like a ball in a bowl). Neutral static stability: after displacement, the aircraft remains in the new state with no tendency to return or diverge. Negative static stability: after displacement, the aircraft tends to continue away from the original state (like a ball on top of an inverted bowl). Certificated aircraft are required to have positive static stability.

cards.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic stability?
cards.answer: Static stability is the initial tendency after a disturbance — does the aircraft tend to return, stay, or diverge? Dynamic stability describes the motion over time. Positive dynamic stability means oscillations decrease and the aircraft eventually returns to equilibrium. Neutral dynamic stability means oscillations continue at the same amplitude. Negative dynamic stability means oscillations increase in amplitude (divergent). An aircraft can be statically stable but dynamically unstable.

cards.question: What are the three axes of flight and which control surface controls each?
cards.answer: Lateral axis (wingtip to wingtip): controlled by the elevator, which produces pitch motion (nose up/down). Longitudinal axis (nose to tail): controlled by ailerons, which produce roll motion (wings bank left/right). Vertical axis (top to bottom): controlled by the rudder, which produces yaw motion (nose left/right). All three axes intersect at the aircraft's center of gravity.

cards.question: What is adverse yaw and how is it corrected?
cards.answer: Adverse yaw occurs during aileron input: the raised aileron on the descending wing creates more drag than the lowered aileron on the rising wing. This extra drag yaws the nose in the opposite direction of the intended turn. It's corrected by applying coordinated rudder pressure in the direction of the turn. The slip/skid ball indicates coordination — keep the ball centered. Some aircraft use differential ailerons or Frise ailerons to reduce adverse yaw.

cards.question: How do flaps affect lift and drag?
cards.answer: Flaps increase the wing's camber and (for some types) effective area, increasing the coefficient of lift and allowing slower flight speeds. They also increase drag. The first increment of flaps adds significant lift with moderate drag increase (useful for short-field takeoffs). Further extension adds proportionally more drag than lift (useful for steeper approaches without increasing speed). Flaps allow steeper descent angles and lower approach speeds, reducing landing distance.

cards.question: What causes a stall to occur at a higher airspeed in a turn?
cards.answer: In a turn, the wing must produce more lift to support both the aircraft's weight and the centripetal force needed for the curved flight path (increased load factor). Since the critical angle of attack remains the same, the wing reaches it at a higher airspeed. Stall speed increases by the square root of the load factor. At 60° bank (2G), stall speed is 1.41× the level-flight stall speed. This is called an accelerated stall and can occur unexpectedly in steep turns.

cards.question: What provides directional (yaw) stability, and what is the contribution of sweep?
cards.answer: The vertical stabilizer (fin) provides directional stability. When the aircraft yaws, the vertical stabilizer presents an angle of attack to the relative wind, generating a sideways force that opposes the yaw and returns the nose to alignment. Wing sweep also contributes: the advancing wing in a yaw has more drag than the retreating wing, creating a restoring moment. The keel effect (side area above vs. below CG) also contributes to directional stability.

cards.question: What provides lateral (roll) stability in most general aviation aircraft?
cards.answer: Wing dihedral (upward angle of wings from root to tip) is the primary contributor to lateral stability. When a wing drops, the aircraft sideslips toward the lower wing. The relative wind hits the lower wing at a higher angle of attack and the upper wing at a lower angle, creating a restoring roll moment. Sweep, keel effect, and weight distribution below the wing also contribute. High-wing aircraft are inherently more laterally stable than low-wing aircraft.

cards.question: What provides longitudinal (pitch) stability?
cards.answer: The horizontal stabilizer (tailplane) is the primary source of longitudinal stability. It acts as a small wing that produces a downward force (in conventional tail designs), keeping the nose from pitching up. The CG location is critical — a forward CG enhances stability; an aft CG reduces it. If the CG moves behind the center of pressure, the aircraft becomes longitudinally unstable and may be unrecoverable. This is why CG limits are strictly enforced.

cards.question: What is the relationship between indicated airspeed and true airspeed at altitude?
cards.answer: Indicated airspeed (IAS) reflects dynamic pressure — what the wing 'feels.' True airspeed (TAS) is actual speed through the air. As altitude increases, air density decreases, so TAS is higher than IAS for the same dynamic pressure. TAS increases approximately 2% per 1,000 ft of altitude. At 10,000 ft, TAS is roughly 20% higher than IAS. This matters for navigation (groundspeed calculations) but not for aerodynamic limits, which are based on IAS.

cards.question: What is the purpose of trim tabs and how do they work?
cards.answer: Trim tabs reduce the control force the pilot must hold for a desired flight condition. A trim tab is a small movable surface on the trailing edge of a primary control surface. When the tab deflects in one direction, it aerodynamically deflects the main control surface in the opposite direction. Elevator trim is most common — it allows hands-off flight at a desired airspeed. Proper trim reduces pilot fatigue and allows smoother, more precise flight.

cards.question: How does the pitot-static system work and what instruments does it power?
cards.answer: The pitot tube measures total pressure (static + dynamic), and the static port measures static pressure only. The airspeed indicator uses both: it measures the difference between pitot (total) and static pressure, giving dynamic pressure, which indicates airspeed. The altimeter uses static pressure only — as pressure decreases with altitude, it indicates higher altitude. The vertical speed indicator (VSI) also uses only static pressure, measuring its rate of change.

cards.question: What happens if the pitot tube becomes blocked?
cards.answer: If the pitot tube is blocked (no drain hole open): the airspeed indicator will freeze at the last reading. If the pitot tube is blocked but the drain hole is open: the ASI will read zero. If both the pitot tube and drain hole are blocked: the ASI acts like an altimeter — reads higher as altitude increases and lower as altitude decreases, because trapped air pressure changes relative to static pressure. Pitot heat should be used to prevent ice blockages.

cards.question: What happens if the static port becomes blocked?
cards.answer: If the static port is blocked, all three pitot-static instruments are affected. The altimeter freezes at the altitude where the blockage occurred. The VSI reads zero. The ASI becomes inaccurate — it reads lower than actual at altitudes above the blockage point (because trapped static pressure is too high) and higher than actual below it. The alternate static source (typically inside the cockpit) provides slightly different readings because cabin pressure is slightly lower than outside.

cards.question: What are the three gyroscopic instruments and what powers each?
cards.answer: Attitude indicator (AI): vacuum-driven (or electric in some aircraft), shows pitch and bank. Heading indicator (HI/DG): vacuum-driven, shows magnetic heading without the oscillation errors of the compass (must be periodically set to the magnetic compass). Turn coordinator (TC): electrically driven, shows rate of turn and coordination (slip/skid ball). Having different power sources (vacuum and electric) means a single system failure won't disable all gyro instruments.

cards.question: What are the magnetic compass turning errors, and what is the ANDS/UNOS memory aid?
cards.answer: When turning through north or south headings, the compass leads or lags due to the dipping tendency of the magnetic lines of flux. ANDS: Accelerate = North indication (compass shows a turn toward north). UNOS: Undershoot North, Overshoot South. When turning to a northerly heading, roll out early (the compass leads); when turning to a southerly heading, roll out late (the compass lags). Acceleration on east/west headings shows a false turn toward north; deceleration shows a false turn toward south.

cards.question: What are the common errors of the magnetic compass?
cards.answer: Variation: difference between true north and magnetic north (corrected by isogonic lines on charts). Deviation: caused by magnetic fields in the aircraft (corrected by the compass correction card). Dip errors: the compass card tilts because magnetic field lines are not horizontal (worst at higher latitudes). Oscillation error: caused by turbulence. Northerly turning error and acceleration error are manifestations of dip error. Always compare compass to heading indicator periodically.

cards.question: How does a carburetor work, and what makes it susceptible to icing?
cards.answer: A float-type carburetor mixes fuel with air using a venturi. As air accelerates through the venturi, pressure drops, drawing fuel through the main jet. The throttle plate controls airflow. Icing occurs because the venturi effect drops temperature (up to 40°F) and fuel vaporization absorbs additional heat (up to 30°F), potentially cooling the air below freezing even when OAT is well above 32°F. Ice blocks airflow, reducing power. Apply carb heat at the first sign of RPM drop.

cards.question: How does carburetor heat work and when should it be applied?
cards.answer: Carburetor heat diverts warm air from around the exhaust manifold into the carburetor intake, raising the temperature above the icing threshold. When applied, expect a slight RPM drop (the warm air is less dense, enriching the mixture). If ice was present, RPM initially drops further, then rises as ice melts. Use carb heat during low-power operations (descents), when icing conditions exist, or whenever you suspect carb ice. Apply full hot or full cold — partial heat can worsen icing.

cards.question: How does a fuel injection system differ from a carburetor?
cards.answer: Fuel injection meters fuel directly into each cylinder's intake port (or into the intake manifold near each cylinder), rather than mixing it upstream in a carburetor. Advantages: more uniform fuel distribution, better fuel efficiency, more power, less susceptibility to induction icing, and easier cold starting. Disadvantages: more complex, harder hot starting (vapor lock), and if the fuel system fails, all cylinders are affected simultaneously. Fuel-injected engines monitor fuel flow rather than mixture indirectly.

cards.question: What is the electrical system in a typical light aircraft?
cards.answer: Most light aircraft use a 14- or 28-volt DC system with an engine-driven alternator (or generator in older aircraft) as the primary power source and a battery as backup. The battery provides power for starting and serves as a reserve if the alternator fails. A master switch has two halves: battery and alternator. A bus bar distributes power to various circuits through fuses or circuit breakers. If the ammeter shows a discharge in flight, the alternator has likely failed.

cards.question: What does the vacuum system power, and what happens if it fails?
cards.answer: The vacuum (suction) system typically powers the attitude indicator and heading indicator. An engine-driven vacuum pump creates suction that spins the gyroscope rotors at high speed. If the vacuum system fails, these instruments will slowly become unreliable as the gyros spin down (the heading indicator drifts, the attitude indicator tilts). The turn coordinator, being electrically driven, remains functional. Monitor the suction gauge — normal is typically 4.5-5.5 inHg.

cards.question: What are the components of a typical light aircraft fuel system?
