cards.answer: A typical fuel system includes: fuel tanks (usually one per wing), a fuel selector valve (allowing tank selection: left, right, both, off), a fuel strainer/drain (lowest point, for checking water/contaminants), fuel lines, an engine-driven fuel pump, an electric auxiliary fuel pump (for priming and backup), and the carburetor or fuel injection system. Always drain fuel sumps during preflight to check for water or contaminants, which appear as bubbles or a distinct layer.

cards.question: What color fuel is 100LL avgas, and why is fuel color important?
cards.answer: 100LL avgas is dyed blue. Other grades: 80/87 is red, 100 (high lead) is green, and jet fuel (Jet-A) is clear to straw-colored. Fuel color is a critical safety check: using the wrong fuel can destroy the engine (Jet-A in a piston engine) or cause detonation (lower grade than required). During preflight, checking fuel color and clarity confirms proper fuel grade and absence of contamination. Water is clear and settles to the bottom of the sample.

cards.question: What are the color-coded arcs and lines on the airspeed indicator?
cards.answer: White arc: flap operating range (Vs0 at the bottom to Vfe at the top). Green arc: normal operating range (Vs1 at the bottom to Vno at the top). Yellow arc: caution range — operate only in smooth air (Vno to Vne). Red line (Vne): never-exceed speed. The bottom of the white arc (Vs0) is stall speed in landing configuration; the bottom of the green arc (Vs1) is stall speed in clean configuration. All speeds are calibrated for max gross weight at sea level.

cards.question: How does the altimeter work, and what errors can occur?
cards.answer: The altimeter is an aneroid barometer calibrated to indicate altitude based on pressure changes. As altitude increases, pressure decreases, and the instrument expands and indicates higher. Errors: incorrect altimeter setting (set too high reads too high — 'high to low, look out below'), nonstandard temperature (cold air is denser, meaning true altitude is lower than indicated in cold conditions), and instrument/position error. Always set the local altimeter before takeoff and update en route.

cards.question: What does the attitude indicator show and what are its limitations?
cards.answer: The attitude indicator (AI, also called artificial horizon) shows the aircraft's pitch and bank relative to the horizon using a gyroscope that maintains orientation in space. Limitations: it may tumble or precess during extreme attitudes (beyond about 100° bank or 60° pitch in some models), after vacuum failure it slowly becomes unreliable, and it has minor precession errors during acceleration and turning. Modern solid-state AHRS-based systems are more reliable.

cards.question: What does the turn coordinator show that the older turn-and-slip indicator does not?
cards.answer: The turn coordinator senses both roll rate and yaw rate, so it shows the rate at which the aircraft is entering or leaving a turn as well as the stabilized turn rate. The older turn-and-slip indicator only shows yaw rate (the rate of turn once established). Both use a ball (inclinometer) to show coordination. A standard-rate turn (2-minute turn, 3° per second) is indicated when the miniature aircraft wing aligns with the index mark.

cards.question: What are the two ignition systems in most piston aircraft engines and why?
cards.answer: Most aircraft piston engines have dual independent magneto ignition systems, each firing its own set of spark plugs (two per cylinder). This provides redundancy — if one magneto fails, the engine continues running on the other. The magneto check during runup verifies both systems work: switching from BOTH to individual magnetos should show a small RPM drop (50-175 RPM typically). No drop may indicate a grounding wire problem; excessive drop indicates a fouled plug or magneto issue.

cards.question: Why should the ignition/magneto switch never be left in the OFF position while hand-propping?
cards.answer: Magnetos are self-contained and generate their own spark independent of the aircraft's electrical system. The ignition switch works by grounding the magneto to disable it. If the grounding wire (P-lead) is broken, the magneto is 'hot' even when the switch is off. For this reason, always treat the propeller as live. During hand-propping, the magneto switch should be on (with a qualified person at the controls) — never assume 'off' means the magneto cannot fire.

cards.question: What is detonation versus pre-ignition in an aircraft engine?
cards.answer: Detonation is the uncontrolled, explosive ignition of the fuel-air mixture after normal spark ignition, caused by excessive heat, low octane fuel, or lean mixture at high power. Pre-ignition is the ignition of the mixture before the spark plug fires, caused by a hot spot (glowing carbon deposit, overheated plug). Both cause extreme temperatures and pressures that can destroy the engine. Correct by enrichening the mixture, reducing power, using proper fuel grade, and ensuring adequate cooling.

cards.question: What are the characteristics and requirements of Class B airspace?
cards.answer: Class B surrounds the busiest airports (e.g., LAX, JFK, ATL). Shaped like an inverted wedding cake from the surface to typically 10,000 ft MSL. Requirements: ATC clearance to enter ('cleared into the Bravo'), two-way radio, Mode C transponder, at least a private pilot certificate (student pilots need specific endorsements for certain Class B airports). VFR visibility minimum: 3 SM, clear of clouds. Cloud clearance requirements are relaxed because ATC provides separation.

cards.question: What are the characteristics and requirements of Class C airspace?
cards.answer: Class C surrounds airports with operational control towers and radar approach control serving a moderate volume of traffic. It typically extends from the surface to 4,000 ft AGL, with an inner circle of 5 NM and an outer circle of 10 NM. Requirements: two-way radio communication established (not clearance — just acknowledgment of your callsign), Mode C transponder. VFR: 3 SM visibility, 500 below/1,000 above/2,000 horizontal cloud clearance. ATC provides separation between IFR and VFR traffic.

cards.question: What are the characteristics and requirements of Class D airspace?
cards.answer: Class D surrounds airports with an operational control tower but without radar approach control. It typically extends from the surface to 2,500 ft AGL, with a radius of about 4 NM (often non-circular to accommodate approaches). Requirements: two-way radio communication established with the tower. VFR: 3 SM visibility, 500 below/1,000 above/2,000 horizontal cloud clearance. When the tower closes, Class D typically reverts to Class E or G.

cards.question: What is Class E airspace and where does it typically exist?
cards.answer: Class E is controlled airspace that is not A, B, C, or D. It exists in many configurations: surface areas around non-towered airports with instrument approaches (designated Class E to surface), the airspace from 1,200 ft AGL up to but not including 18,000 ft MSL (most common), transition areas starting at 700 ft AGL around airports, and Federal airways. No specific entry or communications requirements for VFR, but VFR weather minimums apply. ATC provides separation for IFR traffic.

cards.question: What is Class G airspace and what are the VFR minimums?
cards.answer: Class G is uncontrolled airspace — ATC has no authority or responsibility. It typically exists from the surface up to the overlying Class E (usually 700 or 1,200 ft AGL). VFR minimums vary: daytime below 1,200 ft AGL requires 1 SM visibility and clear of clouds. Night below 1,200 ft AGL: 3 SM, 500 below/1,000 above/2,000 horizontal. Above 1,200 ft AGL but below 10,000 ft MSL: day and night 1 SM visibility and 500/1,000/2,000 cloud clearance (day) or 3 SM (night).

cards.question: What is Class A airspace?
cards.answer: Class A extends from 18,000 ft MSL to FL600 (60,000 ft). All flights must be conducted under IFR, with an IFR clearance. The altimeter is set to 29.92 inHg (flight levels). ADS-B Out is required (as of 2020). VFR flight is not permitted. A pilot must hold at least a private pilot certificate with an instrument rating. Class A exists because the high speeds and altitudes make see-and-avoid impractical.

cards.question: What are the VFR weather minimums for Class E airspace at and above 10,000 ft MSL?
cards.answer: At or above 10,000 ft MSL: 5 SM visibility, and cloud clearance of 1,000 ft below, 1,000 ft above, and 1 SM horizontal. These minimums are higher than below 10,000 ft because aircraft speeds are typically faster at higher altitudes, requiring greater visibility and cloud clearance for see-and-avoid to be effective. These apply in Class E and Class G above 10,000 ft MSL and more than 1,200 ft AGL.

cards.question: What are the types of special use airspace?
cards.answer: Prohibited areas: flight forbidden (e.g., P-56 over the White House). Restricted areas: contain hazards like artillery firing, aerial gunnery, or guided missiles — entry requires permission from the controlling agency. Warning areas: same hazards as restricted but extend over international waters (3+ NM offshore). MOAs (Military Operations Areas): military training, VFR flight is permitted but not advised. Alert areas: high pilot training or unusual activity — no permission needed. CFAs (Controlled Firing Areas): suspend activity when aircraft detected.

cards.question: What is a TFR and how do pilots find information about them?
cards.answer: A Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) is issued via FDC NOTAM to restrict flight in a specific area for a specific time period. Common reasons: presidential movements (VIP TFRs), sporting events, disaster/hazard areas, firefighting operations, and space launch activities. Check TFRs through Flight Service briefings, tfr.faa.gov, or EFB apps. Violating a TFR, especially a VIP TFR, can result in certificate action, interception by military aircraft, and criminal charges.

cards.question: What is the Mode C veil and where does it apply?
cards.answer: The Mode C veil is the area within 30 NM of any Class B primary airport, from the surface up to 10,000 ft MSL. All aircraft operating within this veil must have an operable Mode C (altitude-reporting) transponder and ADS-B Out. This requirement exists even if you never enter the Class B airspace itself. Exceptions: aircraft without an engine-driven electrical system may request deviation, and some older aircraft may request ATC authorization.

cards.question: What is ADS-B and where is it required?
cards.answer: ADS-B (Automatic Dependent Surveillance – Broadcast) Out is required in all airspace where a transponder is currently required: Class A, B, and C airspace, above 10,000 ft MSL (excluding below 2,500 ft AGL), within 30 NM of a Class B primary airport, and above the ceiling of Class B or C. ADS-B broadcasts GPS position, altitude, speed, and identification. ADS-B In (receiving) is not required but provides traffic and weather information to equipped cockpits.

cards.question: What radio communication is required to enter each class of airspace for VFR flight?
cards.answer: Class A: IFR clearance required (no VFR). Class B: explicit clearance ('cleared into Class Bravo'). Class C: two-way radio contact established (controller acknowledges your callsign). Class D: two-way radio contact established with the tower. Class E: no radio contact required. Class G: no radio contact required. The distinction between 'clearance' and 'contact established' is important — in Class C/D, the controller saying your callsign satisfies the requirement even without explicit 'cleared to enter.'

cards.question: What are Federal Airways and what are their dimensions?
cards.answer: Federal Airways (Victor airways) are corridors of Class E airspace based on VOR navigation. They extend from 1,200 ft AGL to 17,999 ft MSL and are 8 NM wide (4 NM on each side of the centerline). They are designated by 'V' followed by a number (e.g., V-16). Jet routes exist from 18,000 ft to FL450 in Class A airspace. GPS-based T-routes are replacing Victor airways for lower altitudes. Knowing airway dimensions helps with airspace planning.

cards.question: Can you fly VFR on top of a cloud layer?
cards.answer: Yes, VFR over-the-top (VFR OTT) is legal as long as you maintain the required VFR cloud clearances and visibility for the class of airspace. However, it carries significant risk: if the cloud layer develops beneath you or thickens, you may not be able to descend VFR. You must still comply with 14 CFR 91.155 VFR weather minimums — 1,000 ft above the cloud tops. VFR OTT requires careful planning and good ADM, as you need a clear descent path.

cards.question: What does 'radar contact' from ATC mean for a VFR pilot?
cards.answer: 'Radar contact' means ATC has identified your aircraft on radar and will provide traffic advisories (workload permitting). It does NOT mean you are in controlled airspace, that ATC has assumed separation responsibility for you (in VFR conditions), or that you are cleared through any airspace. VFR pilots must still see and avoid, maintain VFR cloud clearances, and obtain appropriate clearances. ATC may terminate radar service at any time, especially when workload increases.

cards.question: How is magnetic variation used in navigation?
cards.answer: Magnetic variation (declination) is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north at a given location. It's shown on sectional charts as isogonic lines. To convert true course to magnetic course: in areas of east variation, subtract (East is Least); in west variation, add (West is Best). Example: true course 090°, variation 10°E → magnetic course 080°. This conversion is essential because the magnetic compass reads magnetic headings, while charts measure true directions.

cards.question: What is the difference between true heading, magnetic heading, and compass heading?
cards.answer: True heading is the aircraft's path referenced to true north, corrected for wind (true course ± wind correction angle). Magnetic heading is true heading corrected for magnetic variation. Compass heading is magnetic heading corrected for compass deviation (errors from the aircraft's magnetic fields). The conversion chain: True Course → (+/- wind correction) → True Heading → (+/- variation) → Magnetic Heading → (+/- deviation) → Compass Heading.

cards.question: How does a VOR work, and how do you determine your position using one?
cards.answer: A VOR (VHF Omnidirectional Range) transmits 360 radials. An aircraft VOR receiver compares the phase of two signals to determine which radial (measured FROM the station) the aircraft is on. The CDI (Course Deviation Indicator) shows whether you are left or right of the selected course. The TO/FROM flag indicates whether the selected radial leads toward or away from the station. Two VOR cross-radials give a position fix. Tune, identify (Morse code), and then navigate.

cards.question: What is the difference between pilotage and dead reckoning?
cards.answer: Pilotage is navigation by visual reference to landmarks — identifying features on the ground (rivers, highways, towns, airports) and matching them to the sectional chart. Dead reckoning is navigation by computing heading and groundspeed using known wind, true airspeed, and distance, then tracking position by elapsed time. In practice, VFR pilots combine both: use dead reckoning to fly computed headings and times, and pilotage to confirm position with landmarks. Both require thorough preflight planning.

cards.question: What are the key symbols on a sectional chart that a VFR pilot must know?
cards.answer: Key symbols include: airport symbols (magenta for non-towered, blue for towered), airspace boundaries (blue dashed for Class D, magenta dashed for Class E surface), MEF (Maximum Elevation Figures in quadrangles), VOR symbols (compass roses), obstructions (towers with elevation and height AGL), isogonic lines, terrain contour lines and color-coding (darker colors = higher elevation), MOA/restricted area boundaries, and TFR areas. The chart legend on the back of each sectional details all symbols.

cards.question: What information does the Maximum Elevation Figure (MEF) on a sectional chart provide?
cards.answer: The MEF is shown in each quadrangle (bounded by latitude/longitude lines) and represents the highest known elevation including terrain and obstruction height, plus a safety buffer. It's given in MSL, with the first (large) digit in thousands and the second (smaller) digit in hundreds. For example, '45' means 4,500 ft MSL. MEFs provide a quick reference for terrain/obstruction clearance when detailed chart reading isn't practical. They are not guaranteed obstacle clearance but offer useful guidance.

cards.question: What is compass deviation and how is it corrected?
cards.answer: Compass deviation is the error caused by magnetic fields within the aircraft itself (from the engine, avionics, electrical wiring, etc.). Unlike variation which is location-dependent, deviation changes with the aircraft's heading. It's measured by 'swinging' the compass — comparing compass headings to known magnetic headings on a compass rose. Corrections are recorded on the compass correction card mounted near the compass, showing the deviation for each 30° heading.

cards.question: How is GPS used for VFR navigation and what are its limitations?
cards.answer: GPS provides accurate real-time position, groundspeed, and track information for VFR navigation. It greatly simplifies cross-country flying by showing direct distance, bearing, and time to destination. Limitations: GPS is a secondary navigation aid for VFR unless it's IFR-approved; batteries and electronics can fail; database currency is critical for obstacle and airspace data; and GPS can lead to complacency — pilots may stop scanning for landmarks or monitoring other instruments. Always have a backup plan (paper chart, pilotage).

cards.question: How do you calculate a wind correction angle for cross-country flight?
cards.answer: Using an E6B or electronic flight calculator: plot true course on the chart, apply known winds aloft (direction and speed) to determine the wind correction angle (WCA) needed to maintain your desired track. The WCA is the angle between the true course and the true heading. A crosswind from the left requires a leftward correction (heading more to the left). The E6B also gives groundspeed. Subtract variation to get magnetic heading, then subtract deviation for compass heading.

cards.question: What VOR checks are required for IFR flight and how are they conducted?
cards.answer: Per 14 CFR 91.171, a VOR receiver check is required within 30 days before IFR flight. Methods: (1) VOT — designated test facility, ±4°. (2) Ground checkpoint — designated point at the airport, ±4°. (3) Airborne checkpoint — designated location, ±6°. (4) Dual VOR check — compare two VOR receivers against each other, ±4° between them. (5) Prominent ground feature ±6°. Results must be logged with date, place, bearing error, and signature. Not required for VFR, but good practice.

cards.question: What is a VOR radial vs. a bearing TO a VOR?
cards.answer: A radial is always FROM the VOR station, measured magnetically. The 090 radial is the line extending east from the station. A bearing TO a station is the reciprocal of the radial you're on — add or subtract 180°. If you're on the 270 radial, your bearing TO the station is 090. The OBS setting on the VOR indicator selects a course; the TO/FROM flag tells you whether flying that course takes you toward or away from the station.

cards.question: What is a sectional chart's scale, and how long is it valid?
cards.answer: Sectional charts have a scale of 1:500,000 (1 inch = approximately 6.86 NM). They are the primary chart for VFR navigation. Sectional charts are updated every 56 days (8 weeks). Using an expired chart risks flying with outdated information about airspace boundaries, frequencies, obstacles, and NOTAMs. Terminal Area Charts (TAC) cover Class B areas at 1:250,000 scale (more detail). VFR Flyway Planning Charts are on the back of TACs.

cards.question: What are the four types of hypoxia and their common causes?
cards.answer: Hypoxic hypoxia: insufficient oxygen in the air (altitude). Hypemic hypoxia: blood cannot carry enough oxygen (carbon monoxide poisoning, anemia, blood donation). Stagnant hypoxia: blood circulates inadequately (G-forces, cold, heart conditions). Histotoxic hypoxia: cells cannot use oxygen (alcohol, drugs). All produce similar symptoms: impaired judgment, euphoria, cyanosis, tunnel vision, numbness. Hypoxia is insidious because the first symptom is often impaired judgment, preventing the pilot from recognizing the problem.

cards.question: What are the symptoms and treatment of hyperventilation?
cards.answer: Hyperventilation is excessive breathing that reduces CO2 levels, causing respiratory alkalosis. Symptoms mimic hypoxia: dizziness, tingling in extremities, visual impairment, muscle spasms, and eventually unconsciousness. Critical difference from hypoxia: supplemental oxygen does NOT help (and may worsen it). Treatment: consciously slow your breathing rate, talk aloud, or breathe into a bag to restore CO2 levels. Because symptoms mimic hypoxia, if in doubt at altitude, use oxygen first while also slowing breathing.

cards.question: What is spatial disorientation and how can a pilot overcome it?
cards.answer: Spatial disorientation occurs when the brain receives conflicting signals about the body's position and motion, particularly when visual references are lost (night, IMC). The vestibular system (inner ear) can be fooled — the leans, graveyard spiral, and somatogravic illusion are common examples. To overcome it: trust the instruments, not your body. Do not fly VFR into IMC. If disoriented, focus on the attitude indicator, avoid abrupt head movements, and maintain level flight by instrument reference.

cards.question: What is the IMSAFE checklist and when should it be used?
cards.answer: IMSAFE is a personal health self-assessment before every flight: Illness (Am I sick?), Medication (Am I taking anything that could impair me?), Stress (Am I under significant psychological pressure?), Alcohol (Have I consumed alcohol within 8 hours, or am I still affected?), Fatigue (Am I well-rested?), Eating/Emotion (Have I eaten properly? Am I emotionally fit?). Any 'yes' to a negative condition should ground the pilot. It addresses the #1 cause of accidents: the human element.

cards.question: What is carbon monoxide poisoning, and how can it occur in an aircraft?
cards.answer: Carbon monoxide (CO) is a colorless, odorless gas produced by engine combustion. It enters the cockpit through cracks in the exhaust system, particularly when cabin heat is used (which routes air over the exhaust manifold). CO binds to hemoglobin 200+ times more effectively than oxygen, reducing the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity (hypemic hypoxia). Symptoms: headache, dizziness, drowsiness, cherry-red skin. Treatment: turn off cabin heat, open vents/windows, use supplemental oxygen, and land immediately.

cards.question: What is the recommended wait time between scuba diving and flying?
cards.answer: DAN and the FAA recommend: after a non-decompression dive, wait at least 12 hours before flying above 8,000 ft cabin altitude. After multiple dives per day or multi-day diving, wait at least 18 hours. After any dive requiring a decompression stop, wait at least 24 hours. The reason: nitrogen absorbed under pressure during diving can form bubbles (decompression sickness, 'the bends') when cabin pressure decreases at altitude. Even pressurized cabins may not maintain sea-level pressure.

cards.question: How does night vision work and what techniques help maintain it?
cards.answer: Night vision uses rod cells (peripheral vision, sensitive to light but not color) which take 30 minutes to fully adapt to darkness. To maintain night vision: avoid bright lights (use red cockpit lighting), allow adaptation time, scan using off-center vision (rods are concentrated outside the fovea), keep cockpit lighting dim, avoid staring at a single point (blind spot in center of vision), and supplement with oxygen above 5,000 ft (rods are very sensitive to oxygen deprivation).

cards.question: What visual illusions can affect a pilot during approach and landing?
cards.answer: Runway width illusion: narrow runway = feeling too high (leads to low approach), wide runway = too low (high approach). Upslope illusion: feeling too high (low approach). Downslope: too low (high approach). Rain on windshield: runway appears farther, leading to a steeper approach. Featureless terrain (dark approaches, water): no visual cues leads to flying too low. Bright approach lights: feeling closer, flaring too high. Use VASI/PAPI and altimeter to cross-check visual impressions.

cards.question: What are the effects of medications on pilot performance?
cards.answer: Many OTC and prescription medications can impair pilot performance through drowsiness, dizziness, cognitive impairment, or altered reaction time. 14 CFR 91.17 prohibits operating while using any substance that affects faculties contrary to safety. The FAA publishes a list of approved/prohibited medications. General rules: no sedating antihistamines, no narcotic pain relievers, no psychotropic medications without specific FAA approval. Wait at least 5 times the dosing interval after the last dose before flying.

cards.question: What are the middle ear and sinus problems associated with flying?
cards.answer: As altitude changes, air must equalize between the middle ear/sinuses and the ambient atmosphere. During ascent, trapped air expands and usually vents naturally. During descent, air must flow inward through the Eustachian tube, which is harder — congestion from a cold or allergies can block equalization, causing severe ear pain and possible eardrum rupture. Never fly with an upper respiratory infection or significant congestion. If unable to equalize during descent, climb back up, slow descent rate, and try the Valsalva maneuver.

cards.question: What is Aeronautical Decision Making (ADM)?
cards.answer: ADM is a systematic approach to the mental process used by pilots to consistently determine the best course of action in response to a given set of circumstances. It involves recognizing hazards, assessing risk, and making decisions based on knowledge, experience, and situation analysis rather than impulse. ADM includes risk management, situational awareness, judgment, and the willingness to act — including the decision to not fly. Poor ADM is cited as a factor in most GA accidents.

cards.question: What are the five hazardous attitudes and their antidotes?
cards.answer: Anti-authority ('Don't tell me!') → 'Follow the rules; they are usually right.' Impulsivity ('Do it quickly!') → 'Not so fast. Think first.' Invulnerability ('It won't happen to me.') → 'It could happen to me.' Macho ('I can do it.') → 'Taking chances is foolish.' Resignation ('What's the use?') → 'I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.' Recognizing these attitudes in yourself is the first step toward countering them. Every pilot is susceptible.

cards.question: What is the PAVE checklist for risk assessment?
cards.answer: PAVE helps identify risks in four categories: Pilot (experience, currency, health, stress), Aircraft (performance, equipment, familiarity, fuel), enVironment (weather, terrain, airports, airspace, night), External pressures (deadlines, passengers, 'get-home-itis'). Before every flight, evaluate each element. If any category poses unacceptable risk, mitigate it or cancel the flight. PAVE transforms abstract risk management into a concrete, actionable process.

cards.question: What is the 5P model for in-flight decision making?
cards.answer: The 5Ps provide structured periodic assessment during flight: Plan (is the current plan still working?), Plane (are aircraft systems performing normally?), Pilot (how is my physical and mental state?), Passengers (are they affecting my decisions?), Programming (is the automation helping or adding confusion?). Evaluate the 5Ps at each major phase of flight (preflight, taxi, takeoff, climb, cruise, descent, approach, landing) and whenever conditions change. It promotes continuous situational awareness.

cards.question: What is the risk management process for a planned flight?
cards.answer: Identify hazards (using PAVE or similar), assess the risk level of each hazard (probability × severity), mitigate risks to an acceptable level (change plans, add training, wait for weather, etc.), and accept or reject the remaining risk. If risk cannot be adequately mitigated, the appropriate decision is to cancel or modify the flight. Risk management is continuous — reassess as conditions change. The most dangerous flights often have multiple small risks that compound.

cards.question: What is 'get-there-itis' and why is it dangerous?
cards.answer: Get-there-itis (also called get-home-itis or plan continuation bias) is the overwhelming desire to reach a destination despite deteriorating conditions. It's an external pressure that leads pilots to press on through worsening weather, fatigue, or mechanical issues rather than diverting or delaying. It is one of the deadliest decision-making failures in GA aviation. Antidote: set personal minimums before the flight, identify clear decision points, and have predetermined alternate plans.

cards.question: What is Single Pilot Resource Management (SRM)?
cards.answer: SRM adapts Crew Resource Management concepts for the single-pilot cockpit. It includes: task management (prioritizing and shedding tasks), automation management (effective use of autopilot and GPS), situational awareness (maintaining the big picture), controlled flight into terrain (CFIT) awareness, and effective communication with ATC and passengers. The single pilot must manage all resources without a second pilot for backup, making workload management and decision-making skills critical.

cards.question: What are personal minimums and why should a pilot set them?
cards.answer: Personal minimums are self-imposed weather, experience, and operational limits that are more conservative than regulatory minimums. They account for a pilot's actual skill level and experience rather than the legal minimums written for all pilots. Examples: 'I won't fly if ceiling is below 3,000 ft,' 'I won't fly with crosswinds above 12 knots,' 'I won't fly at night without recent practice.' They should be written down, shared with family, and gradually expanded with training and experience.

cards.question: What is situational awareness and what causes its loss?
cards.answer: Situational awareness is the accurate perception of the factors and conditions that affect the aircraft and flight. Loss of SA often results from: fixation on one task (e.g., troubleshooting a problem while neglecting navigation), fatigue, high workload, complacency on familiar routes, ambiguity (conflicting information), and failure to monitor instruments or surroundings. Warning signs include uncertainty about position, inability to remember recent ATC instructions, and a general feeling that 'something isn't right.'

cards.question: What is the DECIDE model for decision making?
cards.answer: DECIDE is a structured decision-making process: Detect (notice something has changed), Estimate (assess the significance of the change), Choose (determine a desirable outcome), Identify (list possible actions), Do (implement the best action), Evaluate (verify the action is working). It provides a systematic framework when facing unexpected situations, helping pilots move from problem recognition to resolution rather than freezing or making impulsive decisions.

cards.question: What is the standard traffic pattern and its components?
cards.answer: The standard traffic pattern has five legs: Upwind (parallel to runway, direction of takeoff), Crosswind (90° turn from upwind), Downwind (parallel to runway, opposite direction of landing, typically 800-1,000 ft AGL), Base (90° turn from downwind toward the runway), Final (aligned with the runway for landing). Standard patterns use left turns unless otherwise indicated. Enter at pattern altitude on a 45° angle to the midpoint of the downwind leg. Non-towered airports rely on pilot communication and see-and-avoid.

cards.question: What are the right-of-way rules under 14 CFR 91.113?
cards.answer: When converging, the aircraft on the right has right-of-way. Head-on: both alter course to the right. Overtaking: the overtaken aircraft has right-of-way. Emergency aircraft always have right-of-way over all others. The priority order: balloon, glider, aircraft towing/refueling, airship, airplane/rotorcraft. An aircraft in distress has right-of-way over all other traffic. These rules apply when aircraft might conflict — the one with right-of-way should maintain course and speed.

cards.question: What do light gun signals from the tower mean?
cards.answer: For aircraft in flight — Steady green: cleared to land. Flashing green: return for landing. Steady red: give way, continue circling. Flashing red: airport unsafe, do not land. Alternating red/green: exercise extreme caution. For aircraft on the ground — Steady green: cleared for takeoff. Flashing green: cleared to taxi. Steady red: stop. Flashing red: taxi clear of runway in use. Alternating red/green: exercise extreme caution. Flashing white (ground only): return to starting point. Acknowledge by rocking wings (air) or moving ailerons/rudder (ground).

cards.question: What are the standard airport markings for runways?
cards.answer: Runway threshold markings: the beginning of the usable runway. Displaced threshold: arrows leading to the threshold — you can taxi, takeoff, and roll out on the displaced portion, but not land on it. Runway designator numbers: magnetic heading rounded to nearest 10° and divided by 10 (e.g., Runway 27 = ~270°). Centerline markings: alternate 120-ft stripes and 80-ft gaps. Aiming point markings: two broad stripes at 1,000 ft from threshold. Blast pad/stopway: chevrons — not usable for any purpose.

cards.question: What are the different types of airport signs?
cards.answer: Mandatory instruction signs (red background, white text): runway holding position, ILS critical area. Location signs (black background, yellow text, yellow border): identify which taxiway/runway you are on. Direction signs (yellow background, black text): point toward a taxiway intersection. Destination signs (yellow background, black text, arrows): guide to runways, terminal, FBO. Information signs (yellow background, black text): additional advisory info. Runway distance remaining signs (black background, white numbers): thousands of feet remaining.

cards.question: What are VASI and PAPI, and how do you interpret them?
cards.answer: VASI (Visual Approach Slope Indicator) uses two or three bars of lights: red over red = too low, red over white = on glidepath, white over white = too high ('red over white, you're alright'). PAPI (Precision Approach Path Indicator) uses four lights in a row: 4 white = too high, 3 white/1 red = slightly high, 2 white/2 red = on glidepath, 1 white/3 red = slightly low, 4 red = too low. Both typically provide a 3° glidepath. They are especially useful at night or over featureless terrain.

cards.question: What is wake turbulence and how do you avoid it?
cards.answer: Wake turbulence consists of counter-rotating vortices shed from the wingtips of all aircraft producing lift. It is strongest behind heavy, slow, clean (no flaps) aircraft. Vortices sink at 300-500 fpm and drift laterally with wind. To avoid: when landing behind a large aircraft, stay above their glidepath and land beyond their touchdown point. When taking off behind, rotate before their rotation point and climb above their path. Allow at least 3 minutes for vortex dissipation, more in calm winds.

cards.question: What is the proper procedure for collision avoidance scanning?
cards.answer: Effective scanning uses a series of short, regularly spaced eye movements. Focus on each segment of sky for at least 1-2 seconds (the eye needs time to refocus at distance). Scan systematically — side to side or in sectors. Focus on the areas most likely to have traffic (along airways, near airports, at your altitude). An aircraft on a collision course shows no relative motion — it appears as a fixed, growing point. Clearing turns before maneuvers and traffic pattern entry help check blind spots.

cards.question: What radio calls should a pilot make at a non-towered airport?
cards.answer: Use the CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency) and announce: position and intentions when 10 miles out, entering downwind, turning base, turning final, and when clear of the runway. Include: aircraft type, tail number, airport name, position, and intentions. Example: 'Centerville traffic, Cessna 12345, 10 miles to the south, inbound for landing runway 27, Centerville.' End each call with the airport name. Listen before transmitting. Self-announce even if no one else appears to be in the pattern.

cards.question: What is the meaning of the segmented circle at an airport?
cards.answer: The segmented circle is a visual ground-based system at non-towered airports that indicates: wind direction (wind sock, wind tee, or tetrahedron), traffic pattern direction (traffic pattern indicators — L-shaped markers on the segmented circle show the direction of each runway's pattern turn), and the landing runway in use. The large arrowhead of the wind tee or tetrahedron points into the wind (landing direction). The L-shaped indicators extend from the end of the runway to show which direction to turn on downwind.

cards.question: What airport lighting systems should a VFR pilot be familiar with?
cards.answer: REIL (Runway End Identifier Lights): flashing lights at the threshold. MIRL/HIRL (Medium/High Intensity Runway Lights): white along runway edges, yellow in the last 2,000 ft. PAPI/VASI: approach slope indicators. Taxiway edge lights: blue. Rotating beacon: white-green = civilian, white-white-green = military. Pilot-controlled lighting (PCL): key the CTAF mic 7 clicks = high, 5 = medium, 3 = low. Approach lighting systems (ALS) are primarily for instrument approaches but help VFR pilots at night.

cards.question: What transponder codes should a VFR pilot know?
cards.answer: 1200: standard VFR squawk code (no ATC services). 7500: hijack. 7600: communication failure (lost comms). 7700: emergency. A quick memory aid: 75 = taken alive (hijack), 76 = technical fix (radio failure), 77 = going to heaven (emergency). Always squawk 1200 unless assigned a discrete code by ATC. In an emergency, squawk 7700 immediately — it triggers an alert on all ATC radar screens. Never accidentally set 7500, 7600, or 7700 while adjusting your transponder.

cards.question: What is the procedure for communicating with ATC when entering Class C airspace?
cards.answer: Contact approach control on the published frequency (found on sectional charts and Chart Supplement) approximately 20 NM from the airport. State: your callsign, aircraft type, position, altitude, squawk code (if assigned), and intentions. Example: 'Springfield Approach, Cessna 172 N12345, 20 miles southeast at 4,500, inbound for landing with information Delta.' Two-way communication is established when the controller responds with your callsign. If they say 'stand by,' communication IS established. If 'aircraft calling, stand by,' it is NOT.

cards.question: What is the phonetic alphabet and why is it used in aviation?
cards.answer: The ICAO phonetic alphabet (Alpha, Bravo, Charlie, Delta, Echo, Foxtrot, Golf, Hotel, India, Juliet, Kilo, Lima, Mike, November, Oscar, Papa, Quebec, Romeo, Sierra, Tango, Uniform, Victor, Whiskey, X-ray, Yankee, Zulu) is used to prevent miscommunication, especially with similar-sounding letters (B/D/E/P/T). Numbers are pronounced individually: 'Flight level three-five-zero' not 'three-fifty.' Niner replaces nine; tree replaces three. Consistent usage prevents potentially fatal misunderstandings in radio communications.

cards.question: What are the considerations for flying from a high-elevation airport on a hot day?
cards.answer: High density altitude reduces engine power, propeller efficiency, and lift. Key considerations: compute density altitude and check POH performance charts for takeoff distance, climb rate, and accelerate-stop distance. Ensure runway length is adequate with proper margin. Consider departing early morning when temperatures are lower. Lean the mixture for maximum power (per POH, typically full rich below 5,000 ft, lean for best power above). Expect reduced climb rates and plan accordingly for terrain clearance.

cards.question: What is the procedure if you experience a complete engine failure after takeoff?
cards.answer: The immediate priority is to maintain aircraft control — pitch for best glide speed (or Vx if still climbing). Do NOT attempt to turn back to the runway unless you have sufficient altitude (the 'impossible turn' requires at least 800-1,000 ft AGL and is extremely risky). Land essentially straight ahead, making only shallow turns to avoid obstacles. Choose the best available landing site (field, road). Attempt to identify the cause (fuel selector, mixture, carb heat, magnetos). If restart fails, secure the engine, notify ATC (7700), and prepare for forced landing.

cards.question: What is a displaced threshold and how does it affect operations?
cards.answer: A displaced threshold is marked by arrows on the runway surface leading to the threshold. The area before the displaced threshold may be used for taxi, takeoff, and landing rollout from the opposite direction, but NOT for landing. This means: when landing, your touchdown point must be at or beyond the displaced threshold. When taking off from that runway, you may use the entire runway length. The displacement is typically due to obstacle clearance requirements on approach.

cards.question: What should a pilot do if they encounter unexpected IMC while flying VFR?
cards.answer: Immediately execute a 180° turn to return to VFR conditions — this is the most reliable option if recently in VMC. Maintain aircraft control using instruments (wings level, pitch for level flight). Declare an emergency with ATC (squawk 7700). If unable to return to VMC, contact ATC for assistance — they can provide headings, vectors, and approach clearance even without an IFR rating (in an emergency, 14 CFR 91.3 applies). Do not descend unless certain of terrain clearance. This scenario is a leading cause of VFR-into-IMC fatalities.

cards.question: What information is found in the Chart Supplement (formerly Airport/Facility Directory)?
cards.answer: The Chart Supplement provides comprehensive airport information: runway lengths, widths, surface type, lighting, approach aids, elevation, pattern altitude, frequencies (CTAF, tower, ground, ATIS, approach), FBO services, fuel availability, hours of operation, TPA, remarks (noise abatement, special procedures), and nearby NAVAIDs. It's updated every 56 days. It supplements the sectional chart with details that don't fit on the chart. Essential for cross-country flight planning.

cards.question: How does the altimeter setting affect altimeter accuracy?
cards.answer: When flying from high to low pressure without updating the altimeter setting, the altimeter reads higher than the actual altitude — the aircraft is lower than indicated ('high to low, look out below'). Conversely, flying from low to high pressure without updating makes the altimeter read low — the aircraft is actually higher. Each 0.10 inHg error equals approximately 100 ft of altitude error. Always obtain current altimeter settings en route to maintain accurate altitude readings.

cards.question: What should a pilot do when the ammeter shows a continuous discharge?
cards.answer: A continuous discharge on the ammeter indicates the alternator has failed and the battery is providing all electrical power. The battery will eventually deplete. Procedure: try to reset the alternator (per POH — typically cycle the master/alternator switch). If it doesn't reset, reduce electrical load to essentials (radio, one navcom, transponder). Advise ATC and plan to land at the nearest suitable airport before the battery fails. A complete electrical failure means loss of radio, lights, and all electronic instruments.

cards.question: What causes the heading indicator to drift and how often should it be reset?
cards.answer: The heading indicator (directional gyro) drifts due to gyroscopic precession, friction in the gimbal bearings, and the rotation of the Earth. Typical drift is up to 3° every 15 minutes. It should be reset to the magnetic compass every 15 minutes during straight, level, unaccelerated flight (when compass errors are minimized). The DG's advantage over the compass is that it doesn't have turning or acceleration errors, providing a stable heading reference during maneuvering.

cards.question: What are the effects of flying at high altitudes without supplemental oxygen?
cards.answer: Above 10,000 ft, hypoxia symptoms begin for most people: impaired night vision (as low as 5,000 ft), decreased judgment, euphoria, headache. Above 15,000 ft: significant cognitive impairment, time of useful consciousness (TUC) is 20-30 minutes. Above 20,000 ft: TUC drops to 5-10 minutes. Above 25,000 ft: TUC is 3-5 minutes. The insidious nature of hypoxia means pilots may feel fine while their judgment and motor skills are severely degraded. That's why supplemental oxygen rules exist.

cards.question: What is the 'impossible turn' and why is it dangerous?
cards.answer: The 'impossible turn' refers to turning back to the runway after an engine failure shortly after takeoff. It's extremely dangerous because: the turn requires significant altitude (at least 800-1,000 ft AGL depending on conditions), the pilot must make a steep bank turn (increasing stall speed due to load factor), the aircraft is losing altitude throughout, and the turn is 180°+ when accounting for wind and alignment. Most training scenarios show that attempting the turn below a safe altitude results in a stall/spin accident. Landing straight ahead is statistically much safer.

cards.question: How do you properly lean the mixture using EGT?
cards.answer: For cruise flight: slowly lean the mixture while watching EGT rise. When EGT peaks (maximum temperature), you've found stoichiometric (perfect) combustion. For best power (richer mixture), enrichen 50-75°F from peak. For best economy (leaner), lean slightly past peak to 25-50°F lean of peak (only if the engine runs smoothly). Never aggressively lean at power settings above approximately 75% — the lean mixture can cause damaging detonation. Always follow your specific POH procedures.

cards.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled airspace for VFR pilots?
cards.answer: In controlled airspace (A, B, C, D, E), ATC provides separation for IFR traffic and specified services. VFR weather minimums are more restrictive because ATC needs to separate traffic and pilots need to see and avoid. In uncontrolled airspace (G), ATC has no authority. Weather minimums are generally less restrictive (as low as 1 SM clear of clouds during day below 1,200 ft AGL). The practical difference for VFR pilots is mainly the weather minimums and whether communication with ATC is required.

cards.question: What wind information is provided in an ATIS broadcast?
cards.answer: ATIS (Automatic Terminal Information Service) provides current wind direction (magnetic heading), speed in knots, and gusts if present. Example: 'Wind two seven zero at one five, gusts two five.' ATIS is updated hourly or whenever weather changes significantly (a new letter identifier is issued). Pilots should obtain the current ATIS before contacting the tower and state the ATIS identifier. Example: 'With information Delta.' This tells ATC you have current conditions.

cards.question: How does temperature affect altimeter readings, and what is the 'high to low' rule?
cards.answer: Cold temperatures cause the altimeter to read higher than actual altitude because cold air is denser and the pressure levels are compressed closer to the surface. 'From high to low (temperature or pressure), look out below' — your actual altitude is lower than indicated. A common approximation: altimeter error is about 4 ft per degree C deviation from standard per 1,000 ft of altitude. This is especially critical when flying approaches in very cold weather.

cards.question: What causes wake turbulence to be most dangerous during takeoff and landing?
cards.answer: Wake vortices are strongest when the generating aircraft is heavy, slow, and clean (no flaps) — exactly the configuration during takeoff and approach. At low altitudes, there's little room to recover if a vortex rolls or pitches the aircraft. Vortices settle at 300-500 fpm, meaning they descend into the flight path of following aircraft. In calm winds, vortices can linger on the runway for several minutes. Crosswinds can push the upwind vortex onto a parallel runway.

cards.question: What information does the Aeronautical Information Manual (AIM) provide?
cards.answer: The AIM is the FAA's official guide to basic flight information and ATC procedures for use in the US National Airspace System. It covers: navigation aids, lighting and markings, airspace, ATC procedures, emergency procedures, safety of flight (weather, wake turbulence, bird hazards), medical facts, aeronautical charts, and flight planning. While not regulatory (unlike the CFRs), the AIM represents best practices and is testable on the knowledge exam. It is updated periodically.

cards.question: What is an ADIZ and what are the requirements for flying through one?
cards.answer: An ADIZ (Air Defense Identification Zone) is airspace over land or water in which the identification, location, and control of aircraft is required for national security. Requirements: file a flight plan (DVFR for VFR), maintain two-way radio contact, squawk an assigned transponder code, and report to ATC. Deviations or noncompliance can result in military interception. The US has ADIZs along its coastlines. These requirements apply to all aircraft, regardless of size or type.

cards.question: What is the standard rate turn and how do you calculate the bank angle needed?
cards.answer: A standard rate turn is 3° per second, completing a 360° turn in 2 minutes. The bank angle needed depends on true airspeed. Rule of thumb: divide TAS (in knots) by 10, then add half the result. Example: at 100 knots, bank angle ≈ 100/10 + 5 = 15°. At 120 knots ≈ 18°. The turn coordinator's index marks indicate standard rate. In ATC communications, a standard-rate turn is assumed unless otherwise specified. Don't exceed 30° bank in the traffic pattern.

cards.question: What is the significance of Vfe and Vno on the airspeed indicator?
cards.answer: Vfe (top of white arc) is the maximum flap extended speed — exceeding it with flaps deployed can cause structural damage to the flaps and wing. Vno (top of green arc) is the maximum structural cruising speed — the maximum speed in turbulent or gusty conditions. Flying above Vno (in the yellow caution arc) is permitted only in smooth air. Above Vne (red line), the aircraft may suffer structural failure. These speeds are limitations, not suggestions.

cards.question: What is the relationship between bank angle and stall speed?
cards.answer: Stall speed increases with bank angle due to increased load factor. The formula is: stall speed in a turn = level stall speed × √(load factor). At 30° bank (1.15G), stall speed increases ~7%. At 45° (1.41G), ~19%. At 60° (2G), ~41%. This is why steep turns near the ground (traffic pattern, canyon flying) are dangerous — the stall speed may be much higher than expected. The aircraft provides no warning that the 'new' stall speed is higher.

cards.question: What are the effects of frost on an aircraft's performance?
cards.answer: Even a thin layer of frost on the wings can reduce lift by up to 30% and increase drag significantly. Frost disrupts the smooth airflow over the wing, causing early boundary-layer separation. This can prevent the aircraft from becoming airborne at normal rotation speed or cause a stall shortly after takeoff. All frost must be removed before flight — it cannot be 'polished smooth.' There are no exceptions for 'light' frost. Use deicing fluid, hangar heat, or physical removal.

cards.question: What visual cues indicate wind direction at an airport without weather reporting?
cards.answer: Wind sock: points in the direction the wind is blowing (fully extended indicates ~15 knots). Wind tee or tetrahedron: the large end points into the wind (or may indicate the landing direction chosen by the airport). Smoke from nearby sources, dust, wave direction on water, and flag movement all indicate wind direction. The segmented circle may include a wind indicator. Remember to land into the wind for the shortest ground roll.

cards.question: What is density altitude's effect on propeller efficiency?
cards.answer: Propeller efficiency decreases at high density altitude because the propeller is essentially a rotating wing — it produces 'lift' (thrust) from the air. Less dense air means each blade revolution generates less thrust. Combined with the engine producing less power in thinner air, the total thrust output can be dramatically reduced. A normally aspirated engine at 8,000 ft density altitude may only produce about 75% of its sea-level rated power. Always account for this in performance planning.

cards.question: What are the standard NATO phonetic numbers used in ATC communications?
cards.answer: Numbers in ATC communications are spoken individually: 0=Zero, 1=Wun, 2=Two, 3=Tree, 4=Fow-er, 5=Fife, 6=Six, 7=Seven, 8=Ait, 9=Niner. Altitudes/flight levels: 'One two thousand five hundred' or 'Flight level three five zero.' Frequencies: 'One two two point niner five.' Altimeter settings: 'Three zero point zero two.' The modified pronunciations of 3, 4, 5, and 9 reduce confusion over poor radio quality.

cards.question: What are the common causes of controlled flight into terrain (CFIT)?
cards.answer: CFIT occurs when an airworthy aircraft is flown into terrain, water, or obstacles with no awareness by the crew. Common causes: continued VFR flight into IMC, loss of situational awareness (especially at night or over featureless terrain), improper use or interpretation of instruments, failure to maintain minimum safe altitudes, relying on GPS without understanding terrain, and fatigue. Prevention: always know your position, maintain minimum altitudes, use terrain awareness systems, and never continue into deteriorating weather.

cards.question: How does Coriolis force influence weather patterns relevant to a pilot?
cards.answer: In the Northern Hemisphere, Coriolis deflects winds to the right, causing low-pressure systems to have counterclockwise circulation and high-pressure systems to have clockwise circulation. This means: when flying toward a low-pressure system, the wind will shift from your right. The Buys Ballot Law states: in the Northern Hemisphere, if you stand with the wind at your back, low pressure is to your left. This helps pilots estimate pressure center locations relative to wind observations.

cards.question: How can you estimate cloud bases from surface temperature and dewpoint?
cards.answer: Cloud bases can be estimated using the temperature-dewpoint spread formula: cloud base (ft AGL) ≈ ((temperature - dewpoint) / 2.5) × 1,000. This works because rising air cools at the dry adiabatic lapse rate (~3°C/1,000 ft) while the dewpoint decreases more slowly (~0.5°C/1,000 ft), converging at about 2.5°C per 1,000 ft. Example: temp 25°C, dewpoint 15°C → spread = 10°C → 10/2.5 × 1,000 = 4,000 ft AGL estimated cloud base.

cards.question: What is the significance of the 'remarks' section in a METAR?
cards.answer: The remarks section (RMK) provides supplemental data beyond the main body: SLP (sea-level pressure in mb), temperature/dewpoint to tenths of a degree (Txxxx), precipitation amount since last report, lightning location and frequency, variable wind direction, tower/surface visibility if different, and significant weather observed but not at the station (e.g., CB DSNT NE = cumulonimbus distant northeast). RMK data helps pilots get a more complete picture of local weather conditions.

cards.question: What does it mean when the METAR includes '$' at the end?
cards.answer: A dollar sign ($) at the end of a METAR indicates that the automated station (ASOS/AWOS) requires maintenance. The data may still be transmitted but could be unreliable. Pilots should use this observation with caution and seek additional weather information from other sources (nearby stations, PIREPs, or Flight Service). The $ sign is part of the quality control indicators that help users assess the reliability of the observation.

cards.question: How does pressure altitude differ from true altitude and indicated altitude?
cards.answer: Indicated altitude: what the altimeter reads with the local altimeter setting. True altitude: actual height above mean sea level (what matters for terrain clearance). Pressure altitude: altitude indicated when altimeter is set to 29.92 inHg (used for performance charts and above 18,000 ft as flight levels). Absolute altitude: height above the ground (AGL). Density altitude: pressure altitude corrected for temperature (used for performance calculations). On a standard day with correct altimeter setting, indicated and true altitude are equal.

cards.question: What is the purpose of the alternate static source, and how does it affect instrument readings?
cards.answer: The alternate static source provides static pressure when the primary static port is blocked (e.g., by ice). It usually draws air from inside the cockpit, which has slightly lower pressure than outside air due to the venturi effect of air flowing over the fuselage. This causes: the altimeter to read slightly high, the airspeed indicator to read slightly high, and the VSI to momentarily show a climb. The errors are small but should be noted. Some aircraft require breaking the VSI glass as an alternate source.

cards.question: What is the difference between a SPECI and a regular METAR?
cards.answer: A METAR is a routine hourly weather observation. A SPECI (special report) is an unscheduled observation issued when weather conditions change significantly between regular METAR times. SPECI criteria include: ceiling dropping below or rising above specific values (like 3,000, 1,500, 1,000, or 500 ft), visibility changes through 3, 2, or 1 SM, onset/cessation of precipitation or thunderstorms, wind shifts >45°, and other significant changes. SPECIs ensure pilots have timely information about rapidly changing conditions.

cards.question: What is the squawk code to use if you have not been assigned a discrete code by ATC?
cards.answer: VFR aircraft without an ATC-assigned code should squawk 1200. This is the standard VFR transponder code in the United States. When receiving radar services (flight following), ATC will assign a discrete code (e.g., 0432). Always verify the correct code is set and ensure the transponder is on ALT (altitude reporting) mode. Squawking 1200 tells ATC that the radar target is a VFR aircraft not receiving services, helping them prioritize traffic management.

cards.question: How do you determine the traffic pattern altitude for an airport?
cards.answer: The standard traffic pattern altitude (TPA) is 1,000 ft AGL for propeller-driven aircraft and 1,500 ft AGL for large or turbine aircraft, unless otherwise specified. The actual TPA for a specific airport is published in the Chart Supplement. To find it: add the published TPA height to the airport elevation. Example: airport elevation 1,200 ft, TPA 800 ft AGL = pattern altitude of 2,000 ft MSL. Always check the Chart Supplement — many airports use non-standard pattern altitudes.

cards.question: What is the significance of Vle and Vlo speeds?
cards.answer: Vle is the maximum speed with the landing gear extended — flying faster with gear down can cause structural damage to gear doors and mechanisms. Vlo is the maximum speed for operating (extending or retracting) the landing gear — the aerodynamic loads during transition can be higher than with gear stationary. Vlo is typically lower than Vle. In fixed-gear aircraft, these speeds do not apply. Always reduce to below Vlo before moving the gear handle.

cards.question: What are the indications and hazards of a vacuum system failure?
cards.answer: Indications: the suction gauge reads below the normal range (typically 4.5-5.5 inHg), the attitude indicator slowly tumbles or shows unusual indications, and the heading indicator drifts noticeably. The danger is subtle — gyro instruments don't fail instantly but slowly become inaccurate. In IMC, this can lead to spatial disorientation. Cross-check the attitude indicator against the turn coordinator (electric) and verify heading with the magnetic compass. Partial panel procedures should be practiced regularly.

cards.question: What is the difference between sigmet and convective sigmet coverage areas?
cards.answer: SIGMETs cover non-convective severe weather (severe icing, severe/extreme turbulence, volcanic ash, dust/sandstorms reducing visibility below 3 SM) and are valid for up to 4 hours. They apply to all aircraft. Convective SIGMETs specifically cover thunderstorm-related hazards: tornadoes, lines of thunderstorms, embedded thunderstorms, areas of intense thunderstorms, and hail ≥3/4 inch diameter. Convective SIGMETs are valid for 2 hours and cover the eastern, central, or western US.

cards.question: What is the proper procedure for a short-field takeoff?
cards.answer: Position at the very beginning of the runway. Hold brakes, apply full power, check engine instruments, then release brakes. Rotate at the recommended speed (typically Vr from the POH). After liftoff, climb at Vx (best angle) to clear obstacles, then transition to Vy (best rate). Use the flap setting recommended in the POH (typically 10° for short field). Do not retract flaps until obstacles are cleared and a positive rate of climb is established. Back-taxi if available to maximize runway length.

cards.question: What is the proper procedure for a soft-field takeoff?
cards.answer: Keep the elevator full back during taxi to minimize nosewheel loading. Do not stop on the runway — maintain momentum. Apply full power while rolling, and the aircraft will become airborne at a lower-than-normal speed due to ground effect. Hold the aircraft in ground effect (level off just above the surface) to accelerate to Vx or Vy before climbing. Avoid raising the nosewheel too high (could stall) or letting it contact the soft surface (could bog down or nose over). Flaps as recommended by POH.

cards.question: How does wind shear on approach differ from wind shear on takeoff?
cards.answer: On approach with a decreasing headwind shear: airspeed suddenly drops, the aircraft sinks below the glidepath, and the pilot must add power aggressively. On takeoff encountering a headwind-to-tailwind shear: after initial performance increase, airspeed and climb rate suddenly decrease, potentially resulting in terrain contact. The approach case is more dangerous because the aircraft is already slow, low, and in landing configuration with limited excess power. The recommended response to wind shear on approach is an immediate go-around.

cards.question: What does 'established communication' mean for entering Class C and D airspace?
cards.answer: Communication is 'established' when the controller responds to your initial call using your aircraft callsign. If ATC responds with your full callsign, you may enter. If they say 'Aircraft calling, stand by,' communication is NOT established — do not enter. This is a key distinction from Class B, which requires explicit clearance ('cleared into the Class Bravo'). In Class C/D, acknowledgment of your callsign is sufficient, even if followed by 'stand by.'

cards.question: What is the purpose of a holding position sign, and what does it look like?
cards.answer: A runway holding position sign is a mandatory instruction sign with a red background and white inscription showing the runway designation (e.g., '27-9'). It marks the point beyond which aircraft must not proceed without ATC clearance (at towered airports) or without verifying no conflicting traffic (at non-towered airports). These signs protect the runway safety area. Never cross a holding position marking or sign without clearance or positive traffic verification — runway incursions are a serious safety concern.

cards.question: How does a constant-speed propeller work, and what are its advantages?
cards.answer: A constant-speed propeller automatically adjusts blade pitch to maintain a selected RPM. A governor senses RPM and adjusts oil pressure to the propeller hub to change blade angle. Low pitch (flat) = high RPM for takeoff/climb (like a low gear). High pitch (steep) = low RPM for cruise (like a high gear). Advantages: optimized efficiency at all airspeeds, maximum power for takeoff, and fuel-efficient cruise. Controlled by a propeller control lever (blue knob) in addition to the throttle.

cards.question: What are the required reports a pilot must make to ATC without being asked?
cards.answer: Mandatory reports (VFR or IFR receiving radar services): unable to maintain minimum airspeed of 150 knots below 10,000 ft (if requested), loss of nav/comm capability, any safety-of-flight information. For VFR flight following: any altitude change, deviation from route. Additionally, pilots must always report weather conditions (PIREPs) when encountering anything unexpected, especially turbulence, icing, or visibility changes, as this information helps other pilots and forecasters.

cards.question: What are the land and hold short operations (LAHSO) and can a pilot refuse?
cards.answer: LAHSO requires a pilot to land and stop before reaching an intersecting runway, taxiway, or designated hold-short point. ATC issues LAHSO clearances to improve airport capacity. A pilot may decline LAHSO if they are not comfortable with the available landing distance — simply state 'Unable LAHSO.' Student pilots should not accept LAHSO. The available landing distance for LAHSO is published in the Chart Supplement. Pilots must know their aircraft's landing distance to accept safely.

cards.question: How does ADS-B differ from traditional radar surveillance?
cards.answer: Traditional radar uses reflected radio signals (primary) or transponder replies (secondary) to determine aircraft position, with update rates of 4-12 seconds. ADS-B uses GPS-derived aircraft position broadcast once per second, providing more accurate, faster updates. ADS-B doesn't require ground radar stations — just simple receivers. ADS-B In allows equipped aircraft to receive traffic and weather data. ADS-B improves situational awareness in non-radar areas and provides more precise position data for ATC.

cards.question: What happens to an aircraft's airworthiness certificate if an Airworthiness Directive is not complied with?
cards.answer: The aircraft is considered unairworthy and may not be legally operated. Airworthiness Directives are legally enforceable rules, and non-compliance effectively suspends the airworthiness certificate until the AD is satisfied. This applies even if the annual inspection is current — the AD is an independent requirement. Owners/operators are responsible for ensuring all applicable ADs are complied with.

cards.question: What is the difference between a major and minor alteration or repair?
cards.answer: A major alteration or repair is one that might appreciably affect weight, balance, structural strength, performance, powerplant operation, flight characteristics, or other qualities affecting airworthiness. A minor one does not appreciably affect these qualities. Major alterations require FAA-approved data and must be inspected by an IA or authorized repair station before return to service. Minor repairs can be signed off by an A&P mechanic. The distinction determines the level of oversight required.

cards.question: What is the registration requirement for aircraft operating in the US?
cards.answer: All civil aircraft must be registered with the FAA before operation. The Certificate of Aircraft Registration must be carried aboard the aircraft at all times (part of AROW). Registration is valid for 7 years under the re-registration program implemented in 2010. The registration card includes: N-number, aircraft make/model/serial number, and owner information. Transfer of registration must occur within 5 days of sale.

cards.question: What maintenance records must an aircraft owner provide to a buyer at sale?
cards.answer: The seller must transfer: total time-in-service of the airframe, engine(s), and propeller(s); current status of life-limited parts; time since last overhaul of items required to have overhaul intervals; current inspection status (annual, 100-hour, progressive); current status of applicable ADs including method of compliance and AD number; and a list of current major alterations. This ensures the new owner can maintain the aircraft's continuous airworthiness.

cards.question: What pitot-static system tests are required for IFR flight?
cards.answer: The altimeter and pitot-static system must be tested and inspected every 24 calendar months by an appropriately certified repair station. The test verifies that the altimeter indicates within tolerances, that there are no leaks in the system, and that the system meets the requirements of 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix E. While not required for VFR-only flight, maintaining a properly functioning pitot-static system is critical for safety in all operations.

cards.question: What is the 'useful life' requirement for ELT batteries?
cards.answer: ELT batteries must be replaced when 50% of their useful life has expired (as established by the manufacturer) or when the ELT has been used for more than 1 cumulative hour of transmission. The battery replacement or recharge date must be marked on the outside of the transmitter. Using an ELT with an expired battery means the aircraft is not airworthy for flights requiring an ELT. The expiration date should be checked during preflight.

cards.question: What causes lenticular clouds and what do they indicate to pilots?
cards.answer: Lenticular clouds (altocumulus standing lenticularis, or ACSL) form in the crests of mountain waves — standing waves of air on the lee side of mountains. They appear as smooth, lens-shaped clouds that remain stationary despite strong winds. They indicate strong mountain wave activity with potentially severe turbulence, especially in the rotor zone below the clouds. Pilots should avoid flying near lenticular clouds, as turbulence can exceed structural limits.

cards.question: How do virga and precipitation shafts affect flying conditions?
cards.answer: Virga is precipitation that evaporates before reaching the ground, visible as streaks hanging from cloud bases. Hazards include: sudden wind shifts and microbursts caused by the cooling effect of evaporating rain creating strong downdrafts, unexpected turbulence, and reduced visibility. Pilots should avoid flying through or beneath virga, treating it with the same caution as a thunderstorm, because the downdrafts can be as dangerous as those from precipitation reaching the surface.

cards.question: What is the difference between advection and convection in weather?
cards.answer: Convection is the vertical transport of heat by rising warm air — it creates cumulus clouds, thermals, and thunderstorms. Advection is the horizontal transport of air — warm or cold air moving laterally across a surface. Advection fog forms when warm, moist air moves over a cool surface. Understanding the difference helps predict weather: convective activity means unstable air and cumuliform clouds; advection processes explain fog formation, frontal weather, and large-scale temperature changes.

cards.question: What are the conditions needed for a thunderstorm to form?
cards.answer: Three ingredients are required: (1) sufficient moisture in the atmosphere, (2) an unstable lapse rate (temperature decreasing rapidly with altitude), and (3) a lifting action (fronts, orographic lift, convective heating, or convergence). All three must be present simultaneously. Remove any one and thunderstorms cannot develop. This is why thunderstorms are most common on hot, humid summer afternoons — surface heating provides the lift, moisture is available, and the lapse rate is steep.

cards.question: What is the effect of a temperature inversion on wind and turbulence?
cards.answer: A temperature inversion acts as a lid, trapping everything below it (including turbulence, pollutants, and moisture). Wind shear often develops at the boundary of the inversion because winds above and below may differ significantly in speed and direction. Below the inversion, winds may be calm with poor visibility. Above it, visibility is often excellent with smooth air. Pilots climbing through an inversion should expect wind shear, possible turbulence at the boundary, and rapid visibility changes.

cards.question: What weather phenomenon is most commonly associated with warm fronts?
cards.answer: Warm fronts produce extensive areas of stratiform clouds, steady precipitation, low ceilings, and poor visibility that can extend 500+ miles ahead of the surface front. The sequence of clouds as a warm front approaches is: cirrus → cirrostratus → altostratus → nimbostratus → stratus/fog. This progression occurs because the warm air rides up and over the cooler air ahead of it along a gentle slope. IFR conditions may persist for long periods, making warm fronts particularly problematic for VFR pilots.

cards.question: How do you interpret a surface analysis chart's station model?
cards.answer: The station model is a compact plot at each observation station showing: temperature (upper left), dewpoint (lower left), wind (line pointing into the wind with barbs for speed — each full barb = 10 knots, half barb = 5 knots, pennant = 50 knots), sky cover (center circle fill), current weather symbol, pressure tendency (arrow shows rising/falling), and sea-level pressure. Reading station models helps pilots quickly assess conditions across a region without individual METAR text.

cards.question: What weather information is provided by pilot-controlled weather cameras (where available)?
cards.answer: FAA weather cameras (available in Alaska and some CONUS locations) provide real-time or near-real-time images of actual conditions at remote locations. They show visibility, cloud cover, precipitation, and general weather conditions that may not be captured by automated stations. They are especially valuable in areas with sparse weather reporting, helping pilots make go/no-go decisions for routes through mountain passes or remote areas.

cards.question: What does 'P6SM' mean in a METAR or TAF?
cards.answer: P6SM means visibility is greater than 6 statute miles. The 'P' stands for 'plus' or 'greater than.' This is the maximum visibility value reported in a METAR; the system doesn't distinguish between 7 SM, 10 SM, or unlimited visibility. For VFR planning purposes, P6SM means excellent visibility that exceeds all VFR minimums. If a TAF forecasts P6SM, visibility is expected to be well above VFR requirements.

cards.question: What is the relationship between useful load, empty weight, and maximum gross weight?
cards.answer: Useful load = Maximum gross weight − Empty weight. Useful load is everything the pilot adds to the aircraft: fuel, oil (if not included in empty weight), pilot, passengers, and baggage. If maximum gross weight is 2,300 lbs and empty weight is 1,500 lbs, useful load is 800 lbs. The pilot must allocate this among fuel and payload. More fuel means less payload and vice versa. Fuel weighs 6 lbs/gallon for avgas, so 40 gallons = 240 lbs of useful load consumed by fuel.

cards.question: How do you determine the CG shift when weight is added or removed?
cards.answer: CG shift can be calculated by: (weight change × distance from old CG to location of weight change) / new total weight. Alternatively, recalculate the full moment table. Adding weight aft of the current CG moves the CG aft; adding forward moves it forward. This is critical when loading baggage or adding passengers — even small weights at extreme arm positions can shift the CG significantly. Always verify CG remains within published limits.

cards.question: What is the difference between endurance and range?
cards.answer: Endurance is the maximum time an aircraft can remain aloft; range is the maximum distance it can fly. Maximum endurance occurs at minimum fuel flow (lowest power setting that maintains level flight). Maximum range occurs at the speed that covers the most distance per unit of fuel (best L/D speed, similar to best glide speed). Flying faster than best range speed wastes fuel on drag; flying slower wastes fuel on time aloft with little distance gain.

cards.question: How does altitude affect engine performance in a normally aspirated engine?
cards.answer: As altitude increases, air density decreases, providing less oxygen for combustion. Engine power output decreases approximately 3-3.5% per 1,000 ft of density altitude. At 10,000 ft, a normally aspirated engine produces only about 65-70% of its sea-level rated power. The mixture must be leaned to compensate for the thinner air. Turbocharged engines maintain sea-level manifold pressure to a critical altitude, extending high-altitude performance significantly.

cards.question: What is the critical engine on a multi-engine aircraft?
cards.answer: The critical engine is the one whose failure would most adversely affect the aircraft's performance or handling. On conventional twin-engine propeller aircraft with both engines rotating clockwise (as seen from the cockpit), the left engine is critical because its loss results in the most asymmetric thrust and adverse yaw. Some aircraft use counter-rotating propellers, which eliminates the critical engine concept because both engines contribute equally to yaw tendency.

cards.question: How do winglets reduce drag and improve performance?
cards.answer: Winglets (vertical or near-vertical surfaces at the wingtips) reduce induced drag by weakening the wingtip vortices that form as high-pressure air below the wing curls over to the low-pressure area above. By reducing the strength of these vortices, less energy is lost to induced drag. This improves fuel efficiency, climb performance, and range. The benefit is greatest at high angles of attack and low speeds — exactly when induced drag is highest.

cards.question: What is the difference between parasite drag and induced drag at different speeds?
cards.answer: Parasite drag increases with the square of airspeed — doubling speed quadruples parasite drag. Induced drag (a byproduct of lift) decreases as airspeed increases, because at higher speeds a lower angle of attack is needed for the same lift, producing weaker wingtip vortices. Total drag is their sum, forming a U-shaped curve. The bottom of this curve (minimum total drag) is where L/D is maximum — the best glide speed.
