Below this speed, induced drag dominates; above, parasite drag dominates.

cards.question: What would happen if the CG were located behind the aft CG limit?
cards.answer: An aft CG beyond limits makes the aircraft dangerously unstable in pitch. The elevator may not have enough authority to control pitch, especially at low speeds. Recovery from a stall may be impossible because the aircraft naturally tends nose-up. The aircraft could enter an uncontrollable pitch-up and spin. This is why aft CG limits exist — they are determined during certification as the farthest aft position where the aircraft remains controllable in all flight conditions.

cards.question: What are the dimensions and entry requirements of a MOA (Military Operations Area)?
cards.answer: MOAs are depicted on sectional charts with magenta hashed borders. They have defined lateral boundaries and altitude ranges (floor and ceiling). VFR flight through an active MOA is legal without ATC contact, but not recommended due to military training activities including aerobatics, high-speed flight, and formation flying. ATC will route IFR traffic around active MOAs or obtain clearance through them. Check MOA status through Flight Service or the controlling agency listed on the chart margin.

cards.question: What are the requirements and limitations of Class G airspace at night?
cards.answer: At night in Class G airspace below 1,200 ft AGL: 3 SM visibility and 500 ft below, 1,000 ft above, 2,000 ft horizontal cloud clearance (same as daytime Class E). This is stricter than daytime Class G (1 SM, clear of clouds). The increased night minimums recognize that darkness reduces the ability to see and avoid terrain and traffic. Between 1,200 ft AGL and 10,000 ft MSL at night: 3 SM and 500/1,000/2,000. Above 10,000 ft MSL: 5 SM and 1,000/1,000/1 SM.

cards.question: What is a Victor airway and how is it defined on a sectional chart?
cards.answer: Victor airways are Federal airways shown on sectional charts as blue lines with a 'V' designation (e.g., V-16). They are defined by VOR radials and extend from 1,200 ft AGL to 17,999 ft MSL, with a width of 4 NM on each side of the centerline (8 NM total). MEAs (Minimum En Route Altitudes) are listed along the airway. Victor airways are Class E airspace and are used primarily by IFR traffic, but VFR pilots often fly along them for navigation convenience.

cards.question: What are the requirements for flight in a restricted area?
cards.answer: Flight in a restricted area without permission from the controlling agency is prohibited when the area is active. Restricted areas are designated where hazardous activities occur (artillery, aerial gunnery, guided missiles). Activity times are published on sectional charts and NOTAMs. When not active ('cold'), VFR flight through is permitted. Pilots can check status through the controlling agency or Flight Service. Restricted areas are shown on charts with blue hatched borders and 'R' designation.

cards.question: Where does Class E airspace typically begin at airports with published instrument approaches?
cards.answer: At airports with published instrument approaches, Class E airspace typically starts at 700 ft AGL (shown as a magenta dashed line on sectional charts) to protect aircraft transitioning between en route and approach phases. At airports with an operating control tower, Class E may extend to the surface (magenta dashed circle, or it becomes Class D). The 700-ft transition areas ensure IFR traffic has controlled airspace protection during approach and departure.

cards.question: How do you identify a VOR station using Morse code?
cards.answer: Every VOR station transmits a three-letter Morse code identifier that can be heard by turning up the audio on the NAV receiver. You must identify the VOR before using it for navigation to confirm you're tuned to the correct station — a VOR undergoing maintenance may transmit without an identifier, or you may have tuned the wrong frequency. The identifier is also shown on sectional charts and the Chart Supplement. The T-E-S-T signal indicates a VOT (VOR test facility).

cards.question: What does the CDI (Course Deviation Indicator) on a VOR tell you?
cards.answer: The CDI needle shows displacement from the selected course. Each dot represents 2° of deviation, with full-scale deflection at 10°. If the needle is left of center, the selected course is to the left (fly left to intercept). The TO/FROM flag tells direction to the station relative to the selected course. Important: CDI sensitivity is distance-dependent — at 60 NM, one dot = 2 NM; at 6 NM, one dot = 0.2 NM. Always check both the CDI and TO/FROM flag together.

cards.question: How do you determine your position using two VOR stations?
cards.answer: Tune and identify the first VOR, center the CDI with a FROM flag, and note the radial. Repeat with a second VOR. Your position is where the two radials intersect on the chart. For best accuracy, choose stations that produce radials crossing at close to 90° (between 60° and 120° is acceptable). Radials closer to parallel give an elongated area of uncertainty. This cross-radial fix technique was the primary means of VFR position verification before GPS became widespread.

cards.question: What is the difference between a VOR, VOR/DME, and VORTAC?
cards.answer: A VOR provides azimuth (direction) information only. A VOR/DME adds Distance Measuring Equipment, so the aircraft can determine both direction from and distance to the station. A VORTAC combines VOR azimuth with military TACAN distance, providing the same VOR/DME functionality to civilian aircraft. On sectional charts, a VOR has a simple compass rose, a VOR/DME has a small square in the corner of the symbol box, and a VORTAC has a three-sided symbol.

cards.question: How does latitude and longitude work for navigation on a sectional chart?
cards.answer: Latitude lines run east-west (measured 0-90° north/south from the equator). Longitude lines run north-south (measured 0-180° east/west from the Prime Meridian). Each degree is divided into 60 minutes; each minute of latitude equals approximately 1 nautical mile. GPS coordinates use this system. On a sectional chart, the grid lines are spaced at 30-minute intervals. Latitude/longitude is used for plotting GPS waypoints, reporting position, and measuring distance (using the latitude scale on the side of the chart).

cards.question: What is the 'somatogravic illusion' and when does it typically occur?
cards.answer: The somatogravic illusion occurs during rapid acceleration (such as takeoff or a go-around), where the body interprets the forward acceleration as a pitch-up. The pilot may instinctively push the nose down, potentially flying into the ground. During rapid deceleration, the opposite occurs — the pilot feels nose-down and may pull up, potentially stalling. This illusion is most dangerous at night or in IMC when visual references are absent. Trust the instruments, not the body's sense of pitch.

cards.question: What is the 'leans' illusion in aviation?
cards.answer: The leans occur when a pilot enters a gradual bank unnoticed (below the vestibular threshold). Upon suddenly noticing and correcting the bank, the semicircular canals sense the correction as a roll in the opposite direction. The pilot feels like they are banking when actually wings-level, and instinctively leans toward the original bank. It's the most common form of spatial disorientation. The cure: trust the instruments and resist the urge to lean or re-enter the bank.

cards.question: What is the effect of fatigue on pilot performance?
cards.answer: Fatigue degrades nearly every cognitive and physical skill needed for flying: reaction time slows, judgment deteriorates, attention narrows, situational awareness decreases, and risk tolerance inappropriately increases. Acute fatigue (short-term) can be resolved by rest; chronic fatigue (ongoing sleep deficit) requires sustained recovery time. Studies show 17 hours without sleep impairs performance equivalent to a 0.05% BAC. The FAA and IMSAFE checklist both emphasize fatigue as a critical go/no-go factor.

cards.question: What is normalization of deviance and how does it relate to aviation safety?
cards.answer: Normalization of deviance occurs when pilots gradually accept increasingly risky behaviors because they've 'gotten away with it' before — flying with marginal fuel, skipping checklist items, or pushing weather limits. Each successful outcome reinforces the risky behavior. Over time, what was once recognized as risky becomes routine. It's countered by maintaining disciplined adherence to procedures, personal minimums, and standards regardless of past outcomes. Just because nothing bad happened doesn't mean the risk wasn't real.

cards.question: How should a pilot evaluate and manage external pressures?
cards.answer: External pressures include: passenger expectations, business deadlines, desire to return home, peer pressure, and sunk costs (fuel/hotel already paid). These pressures cloud judgment and push pilots toward continuation bias. Mitigation strategies: make the go/no-go decision before getting to the airport, set personal minimums in advance, always have an 'out' (alternate airport, next-day plan), involve passengers in decision-making ('I want to keep us safe'), and remember that a late arrival beats no arrival.

cards.question: What is the 'Swiss cheese model' of accident causation?
cards.answer: The Swiss cheese model (James Reason) illustrates how accidents result from multiple layers of defense each having holes (weaknesses). Individually, no single hole causes an accident — but when the holes align across multiple layers (pilot error, mechanical issue, weather, organizational factors), the hazard passes through all defenses and an accident occurs. The model emphasizes that breaking any link in the chain prevents the accident. Good ADM, maintenance, training, and regulations are all 'slices' of cheese.
